I, surprisingly, did some research into this. While it is easy to get colors in this part:

```
... E create an object with a uuid
... ✗ get that uuid object

ERROR: create an object with a uuid
	Unable to find the server at stub
FAIL: get that uuid object
	unable to replace $LOCATION in $LOCATION, data unavailable: 'input_create_an_object_with_a_uuid' object has no attribute 'location'
```

It is not in this part:

```
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Ran 2 tests in 0.006s

FAILED (failures=1, errors=1)
```

The former is driven by TextTestResult but the latter is part of TextTestRunner (in its very long `run` method). Changing run to use colors would mean duplicating that long method, which doesn't have a good shelf life.

What I currently have as a prototype is some code that will turn the ✔ green and the E and X red. Which is pretty but doesn't quite have the same impact.

![screen shot 2015-08-06 at 15 46 27](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/34498/9114540/56c01c16-3c52-11e5-81e1-f09f1e78a20d.png)

https://developer.microsoft.com/en-us/microsoft-edge/tools/vms/

(Note: Microsoft says that their [testing VM license terms](https://modernievirt.blob.core.windows.net/vhd/release_notes_license_terms_1_5_15.pdf) supercede the trial-version license within the VM and explicitly recommends that you use your VM manager to snapshot the VM before first boot so you can easily reset the trial period. The gist of the license is that it has their usual EULA boilerplate about making copies, but, instead, says that you are not allowed to activate the contained copies of Windows and must only use them for testing things.)

@rschwiebert, to make sure that flower has indeed got events from workers, you could add a log `logger.info('---> event: %s, worker: %s', event_type, worker_name)` before [line 42](https://github.com/mher/flower/blob/master/flower/events.py#L42).

Also, you could start flower with `--logging=debug` to get a verbose log.

I think conda maintains consistent dependency within an environment, while pip seems to just ensure that the most recent thing you install has its requirements fulfilled. i.e. A needs numpy < 1.12, while B needs >=1.12. Pip will happily let you install A, then B. Conda will complain.

Also conda uninstall is guaranteed to work (i.e. remove all the packages' files), while IIRC pip is not.

Given a multi-part index, minimap2 aligns all query sequences against each part in turn. It doesn't know sequences in other parts and assigns mapping quality based on sequences in one part only. This is why your highlighted read got mapQ=60 twice, though clearly the chr17 hit is the optimal one.

> Can the output from a multi-part index be merged such that it is exactly the same as the output for the single whole index?

Unfortunately, no. As it is often impractical to load all query into memory, doing this would require minimap2 to save the exact internal states of each query to disk. This will involve a lot of efforts and code refactoring.

A possibly easier solution is to save sam/paf mapped against to each part to a separate file and then merge them back at the end. However, mapping quality calculated this way will sometimes differ from mapQ computed from a uni-part index. In addition, this strategy is still tricky to implement.

> If not is there any reduction in accuracy when the multi-part index is used?

When you use a multi-part index, I believe accuracy is not reduced as long as you merge alignments to each part at the end. However, the results will be different in corner cases.

> Are there any existing scripts/methods to perform this merging?

Unfortunately, no. I know your intended applications. However, minimap2 is not designed for those. Multi-part index is mostly used for read overlapping or searching hits against gigantic databases (e.g. nt) when you care about all local hits.

Dear @zbeekman,

I just look with more attention to this issue. I am a bit confused.

In my original intention `-mklib` should not add `-c`: it is indented to check some other building stuffs specific of library creation, e.g. running `ar`. The `-c` flag is the default setting of all supported compilers expect the custom one where must set all. Into your invocation you explicitly pass a value for cflags, i.e. `-cflags  ' -O0'` that override the default one, purging out the default `-c`. This should be happen also for building a program.

Now I have some possibilities:

1. leave this behavior as it is now, maybe improving the documentation pointing out that specifying a user `cflags` override the default value purging out the `-c` if not directly inserted into the user value;

2. let the user to add his/her own value to the default one (that I guess it is the behavior you was expecting), thus in an invocation like your the resulting `cflags` value is `cflags = -c -O0` where `-c` is the default value and `-O0` is the addition of the users;

3- explicitly check/parse the final `cflags` for the presence of `-c`; this should result the final value of the approach 2. until the default value is always `-c`.

Let me know what you prefer.

See you soon.

import gooeyGUI as gg
import subprocess
import sys
HOW_DO_I_COMMAND = 'python C:\\Python\\PycharmProjects\\GitHub\\howdoi\\howdoi\\howdoi.py'

def HowDoI():
    '''
    Make and show a window (gooeyGUI form) that takes user input and sends to the HowDoI web oracle
    Excellent example of 2 new GUI concepts
        1. Output Element that will show text in a scrolled window
        2. Non-Window-Closing Buttons - These buttons will cause the form to return with the form's values, but doesn't close the form
    :return: never returns
    '''
    # -------  Make a new FlexForm  ------- #
    form=  gg.FlexForm('How Do I ??', AutoSizeText=True, DefaultElementSize=(30, 2), Icon=DEFAULT_ICON)
    form.AddRow(gg.Text('Ask and your answer will appear here....', Size=[40,1]))
    form.AddRow(gg.Output(Size=(90, 20)))
    form.AddRow(gg.Multiline(Size=(90, 5), EnterSubmits=True),
                gg.ReadFormButton('SEND', ButtonColor=(gg.YELLOWS[0], gg.BLUES[0])),
                gg.SimpleButton('EXIT', ButtonColor=(gg.YELLOWS[0], gg.GREENS[0])))

    # ---===--- Loop taking in user input and using it to query HowDoI web oracle --- #
    while True:
        (button, value) = form.Read()
        if button == 'SEND':
            command = value[0][:-1]
            QueryHowDoI(command)
        else:
            print(button, 'pressed')
            break

    print('Exiting the app now')
    exit(69)

def QueryHowDoI(Query):
    '''
    Kicks off a subprocess to send the 'Query' to HowDoI
    Prints the result, which in this program will route to a gooeyGUI window
    :param Query: text english question to ask the HowDoI web engine
    :return: nothing
    '''
    howdoi_command = HOW_DO_I_COMMAND
    t = subprocess.Popen(howdoi_command + ' '+ Query, stdout=subprocess.PIPE)
    (output, err) = t.communicate()
    print('You asked: '+Query + 'The HowDoI Oracle says:\n')
    print(output.decode("utf-8") )
    exit_code = t.wait()

if __name__ == '__main__':
    HowDoI()
```

> maybe it's best to drop the bracket notation altogether

That, I'm not going to do, because, well, it *is* semantically a dict.  And, `foo[some_predefined_name]` is IMO clearer than `getattr(foo, some_predefined_name)`.

> The alternatives are either backwards-incompatible...

That's the thing.  Yes, it is backward compatible, but only for the getter.  The setter already has this check...  OTOH, people do a lot more getting than setting, so yeah, things will probably break.

> I think the best approach would be to coerce the name to a PdfName object.

I'm certainly leaning that way...

That's what I ended up doing, but I still think the difference between bracket getters and attribute getters is unnecessarily confusing, especially due to the fact that if you do something that _should_ have no effect, like reading the keys, it suddenly changes the behaviour of the getter. Especially since that is typically one of the first things one would add when debugging, and when removing that you don't want the code to break.

Given that you're on the fence on what to do, I think the best approach would be to coerce the name to a `PdfName` object. The alternatives are either backwards-incompatible (if you decide to give an error when passed a string) or keeping the confusing status quo. If you don't mind breaking backwards compatibility, maybe it's best to drop the bracket notation altogether. Maybe I'm missing something, but to me it looks like redundant syntax for the same thing which nevertheless acts differently.

Thanks for pointing out that the tuple syntax doesn't require parentheses. Still, I find it clearer to add them anyway since there are situations where they _are_ required (in method calls, for example), and having a consistent syntax for them is less mental parsing overhead.

All metrics recording have the implicit concept of a quantum of time.
In the case of StatsD, the server handles aggregating these metrics, potentially from separate clients.
In the case of CloudWatch, this also happens somewhere; ie., the service does not store all the individual increments of a metric, just a value for a period.
In the case of logging, it does not happen.
In the case of logging-rollup, metrics are managed internally and aggregated for output periodically.

So, this is what I'd do:

StatsD handles this.
In the case of CloudWatch, I don't know this system, perhaps there's something. Otherwise, do as in logging-rollup.

In the case of logging-rollup, keep a set of values for the period to be rolled up. This backend is nice.

If I'm not mistaken, P.1812 includes the clutter into the terrain heights of the path (plus some endpoint correction). Applying P.2108 would then do an additional clutter correction, which likely leads to completely overestimated clutter attenuation. Of course, if you don't add clutter heights to the terrain, you could do that. 

However, I'd rather modify the output of the `height_map_data` function - effectively adding the clutter heights to the “height_profs” array. (I'll show you how to do that.) Of course, one needs a database for the clutter in the area of interest. However, as I said, one needs clean topographic data to start with - and SRTM is not clean. An alternative could be to assume that SRTM already contains topography + clutter and just work with that (no additional correction). I'll leave it up to you, which is better for your application. In any case, I don't have access to any other GIS data than SRTM.

Not to derail this thread, but just a thought: 

Since ds4drv only supports a single type of device, It might be better to remove the python-evdev dependency. The evdev interfacing can be done in Python with ctypes, removing the need to have gcc installed to build the python-evdev dependency. This may open up ds4drv to working on more embedded or SOC devices as a bonus. The pyglet library does things this way, and works great. 

Don't get me wrong, python-evdev is a fantastic library. But, it might be a bit overkill for ds4drv. Not that writing the code for ds4drv would be easy, but it could be mostly learned from studying pyglet, as the license allows that.

It's possible to modify the code so that the ds4drv joystick device is added and removed dynamically, but I think it would be easier if you just detect whether the actual ds4 joystick device is present. Perhaps you could just poll `/proc/bus/input/devices`.

Thanks for raising an issue. Some brief investigation suggests you have found 2 bugs in PushButton:
 - _on_release() should not call _command() if the state is disabled
 - toggle() only checks for "normal" and "disable" states, if the state is "active", which it is when the button has been 'pressed' it doesn't toggle to "disable"

I'll include fixes in the next release.

#camusdv, I checked out w you said about the using the PCM fifo to source the DMA_REQs. I can confirm that the pulse width does not double on this case.

So to summarise.

1. With the default PWM fifo as the timing (DMA_REQ) source using more than dma channels (dma engines) will cause pulse increment to double thus doubling the length of the subcycle and any pulses in it.
2. Using the PCM fifo as the timing source having more than one dma channel works as expected.

So if you want to use more than DMA channel then you must use the PCM fifo as the clock source. Maybe the code should be modified to enforce this.

As to why this is happening I have no clue! I cannot see any difference in the broadcom spec between DMA_REQ line 5 and DMA_REQ line 2, on either the DMA engine side or the peripheral side.

@gdmcbain By `metadata` I mean a special dict of something that is very specific to the format that you are reading from or writing to. For example, if you read gmsh and next write vtk, the vtk writer can just ignore this metadata, but if you write back gmsh, then you honor it. The use case is doing some pre-processing on the input mesh data, and writing it back in the same format. Of course, it is up to the user to doing the read-transform-write to update the metadata if needed.

We found the installation in Windows 10 (64-bit) to be tricky but were ultimately able to get it to work. We used IntelliJ IDEA with a Python plugin. Instead of Anaconda, we use WinPython on Windows. Please see instructions below, if you'd like to give it a try.

1. Download [WinPython 3.5.2.2](https://sourceforge.net/projects/winpython/files/WinPython_3.5/3.5.2.2/WinPython-64bit-3.5.2.2.exe/download).
2. Download [iPython 4.2.1](https://github.com/ipython/ipython/archive/4.2.1.zip).
3. Download [Gdspy 1.0](https://github.com/heitzmann/gdspy/releases/download/v1.0/gdspy-1.0.win-amd64-py3.5.exe).
4. Open ```WinPython Control Panel.exe``` and uninstall the latest ipython package.
5. In the control panel, install iPython 4.2.1 and Gdspy 1.0.
6. Append the path to ```python.exe``` (for example, C:\Users\USERNAME\WinPython-64bit-3.5.2.2\python-3.5.2.amd64) to the PATH Windows environmental variable.
7. Restart IntelliJ IDEA (tested with version 2016.2.4)
8. Verify installation by invoking ```import gdspy``` in the IDEA python console.

No, this is just part of the extractor-"infrastructure" used by gallery-dl and independent of the language used. The important line for URL-delegation is line 40: (`deviation`, in this case, contains a folder- or collection-URL)

https://github.com/mikf/gallery-dl/blob/9aa95fba8c0415ad0232a67649ae9f32bfdc81e9/gallery_dl/extractor/deviantart.py#L39-L41

... which then ends up here ...

https://github.com/mikf/gallery-dl/blob/9aa95fba8c0415ad0232a67649ae9f32bfdc81e9/gallery_dl/job.py#L136-L140

... and starts a new download job using another sub-extractor.


> if it would also be possible to delegate by calling a sub-extractor class from inside another sub-extractor class?

That would actually be possible, but it would use the settings and formats of the "parent"-extractor, which might or might not be useful.

This would allow for different configuration options to be a possibility.

@wrolf you might be able to emulate the traffic using a `--pre-hook`. It's in the latest version on pypi. Your pre-hook can even read the log script and update the params for boom accordingly.

You can access a global var in your pre-hook function that does everything you need. Here's an example that I did recently.

```python
import random

i = 0


def pre_hook(method, url, options):
    global i
    if i % 3 == 0:
        options['headers']['Authorization'] = 'Token f848c2795880356aabdeacd1a8fabb6bec56b78e8'
    else:
        options['headers']['Authorization'] = "Token {}".format(''.join(random.choice('0123456789abcdef')
                                                                        for c in range(40)))
    i += 1
    return method, url, options
```

Notice how i'm updating the header and incrementing `i` so i can make every 3rd request use a real token but otherwise use a bogus token. You might be able to do something similar by traversing through your log file and updating the `method, url, options` vars returned from the pre-hook accordingly. 

Hope this helps!

### Short version
As a very beginner with python and `asyncio` I would start with a normal `telepot.Bot` and move to a `telepot.aio.Bot` if the performance is far lower than expected.
Where _performance_ refers to the interaction with external services such as doing an api-request at  Telegram.

### Long version
Telegram only allows one long-polling request per api-key for `Update`s, which include messages, InlineQuerys, etc. Which is next to the `GIL` of python your main limitation.
You can use [`asyncio`](https://docs.python.org/3.5/library/asyncio.html) and `telepot.aio` to do things in parallel: e.g. receiving and sending data - but be warned: `asyncio` is quite complex and your script can be [_at multiple positions at the same time_](https://docs.python.org/3.5/library/asyncio-task.html#example-chain-coroutines).
That being said the access on other resources may be done with more care, as multiple instances of a message handler could access them.

Yeah - people will be upset either way. I think it boils down to which side of the divide is larger? Folks who use `now()`? Or folks who use `utcnow()`?

Either way - we can respect the system timezone fairly simply with:

```python
import time
time.tzname
```

If you provide `freeze_time('2015-01-16T20:00:00+00:00')` or any other time with a timezone - code should work either way.

The tricky part is naive `freeze_time('2015-01-16T20:00:00')`. People 100% have to workaround this bug now if you mix `now()` and `utcnow()` as it diverges - so fixing it means we 100% will break stuff. So, just gotta break stuff in the right direction. :-)

I did some more Googling for what belongs in data versus cache, I think it's fine to remain in XDG_DATA_HOME.

Based on [GNOME's recommendation](https://wiki.gnome.org/action/show/Initiatives/GnomeGoals/XDGConfigFolders):

> * If you remove the .cache folder, the user MUST not notice it except maybe for performance (might be huge performance drop)
> * .local should be treated as any other user data

And [Debian's recommendation](https://wiki.debian.org/XDGBaseDirectorySpecification):

> The above example information is not essential data. However it should still persist on reboots of the system unlike cache data that a user might consider putting in a TMPFS.

So it seems to be that the cache directory is for anything the program can regenerate without user intervention, and the data directory is for anything else.

I would not expect `nflgame.update_sched.run()` to work inside of a REPL. It's a command line application.

I'm not really familiar with iPython. The fact that `nflgame.one` is giving you a `None` value simply means that you don't have the updated schedule. If you're just running `nflgame-update-schedule`, then maybe it's already trying to pull from the conference championship and not for the divisional round. You can force it to update for a specific week:

```bash
nflgame-update-schedule --year 2014 --phase POST --week 2
```

Otherwise, I don't really know what the problem is. If running code outside of iPython is working just fine and can access the divisional round games, but the *same exact* code fails in iPython, then I see two possibilities:

1. You have two different versions of `nflgame` installed and for whatever reason, iPython is using version A and everything else is using version B.
2. iPython is doing something weird with Python paths that I don't understand. Maybe `__file__` isn't working? Not sure.

To check for #1, try running this in your terminal:

```bash
[andrew@Liger ~] python2 -c 'import nflgame; print nflgame.__path__'
['/home/andrew/data/projects/nflgame/nflgame']
```

Then try this in your iPython shell that's causing problems:

```python
import nflgame
print nflgame.__path__
```

Do the two paths printed match? If not, then you have two different installs of `nflgame`.

You know the tree nodes are stored as Python dict, which only reserves hashed order. It cann't be ordered on the tree per se. 
I think there is a small mistake in ur code. `children` is a local variable as a list returned by tree.children. Its sorted and then dropped before entering next iteration.

The Tree stores the nodes in a dictionary, which does not preserve the order. On tree output the child nodes are sorted by their id.

I'm kinda against this. Not to the point of rejecting it out of hand, but not supporting Python 2.6 is something of a deliberate choice on my part. I wrote a bit about it here: http://www.drmaciver.com/2015/05/if-you-want-python-2-6-support-pay-me/

I'll probably accept a PR with 2.6 support, but I'd caution against your putting a bunch of time into this if you haven't already got it working, as you're probably underestimating how much work it is (and if you *have* already got it working, good job. Also, if you do get 2.6 support working I make no promises about keeping it in. I'll basically keep it in as long as it's easy to do, but supporting a wide range of Python versions is in the top 2 or 3 most annoying things about Python library development, and if 2.6 significantly adds to that burden it's probably going to get dropped at some point.

Unfortunately the fix will have to be quite intrusive, because I think what needs to be done simply can't be done with JSONSerializer.  That's because it's a format which doesn't correctly preserve the types of the strings inside a python dict.

on python2, it's data destructive:

```
>>> d = {'key1': 'val1', 'key2': u'val2'}
>>> d
{'key1': 'val1', 'key2': u'val2'}
>>> json.loads(json.dumps(d))
{u'key1': u'val1', u'key2': u'val2'}

```

on python3, it bails out (more correct, but makes betamax fail hard):

```
>>> d = {'key1': 'val1', 'key2': b'val2'}
>>> d
{'key1': 'val1', 'key2': b'val2'}
>>> json.loads(json.dumps(d))
TypeError: b'val2' is not JSON serializable

```

Ah. Strings are generally considered "docstrings" only when used, as the first statement of a Python construct, to document that construct. That's why I didn't understand.

Parsimonious's grammars are expressed as strings in order to make them look as much like PEGs (or even EBNFs, sort of) as possible without having to do a lot of roundabout coding to wedge them into a Python-object style. PyParsing does the latter, and the result is, to my eyes, less readable. It also prevents you from making forward references without predeclarations, since Python would complain of NameErrors.

I don't know of any editor that would, out of the box, do useful highlighting on a Parsimonious grammar even if they weren't contained in strings. However, most popular editors are sufficiently customizable that you could probably put something together, even given the string containers. I'd be very interested to see anything you come up with!

      "@type": "UserOutgoingCallingPlanOriginatingModifyRequest"
      }, 
      "#text": ";"
    }
  }
}"""

PARSED = xmltodict.parse(XMLSTRING.strip(), process_namespaces=True, namespaces={'C':None, 'xsi':None, 'http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema-instance':None})

RESULT = json.dumps(PARSED, indent=2)

if RESULT != EXPECTED:
    print('EXPECTED')
    print(EXPECTED)
    print
    print('RESULT')
    print(RESULT)

    print
    print('DIFF')
    for line in difflib.unified_diff(RESULT.splitlines(1), EXPECTED.splitlines(1), fromfile="RESULT", tofile="EXPECTED"):
        sys.stdout.write(line)
    sys.exit(1)
```

I understand your motives. As a programmer it is very tempting to hack a hackable language. I've hacked the sh*t out of python, C++ operator overriding, template metaprogramming, etc...

It took me more than a decade to realise that in case of large codebases it is much easier to navigate and read the code if I don't have to dig deep to find out how some deeply hidden magic works. It is a best case scenario when you can at least find out by reading the code that there is hidden magic. The much worse scenario is when you can't even see it by reading the code. E.g.: your `with` implementation, or for example an overridden `__add__` operator that doesn't behave as normally expected and does something fancy.

I've also learned that abstraction is super dangerous. If you aren't convinced that an abstraction is useful then don't introduce it. Sometimes writing a bit bloaty code or copy-pasted code is less expensive than introducing the wrong kind of abstraction (that will be very difficult to eliminate/cleanup/refactored compared to bloat or copy-paste). An abstraction first has to be understood, then people have to realise that it is the wrong abstraction, then a strategy is needed to remove it... In case of bloat and copy-paste: it is bloody obvious how to deal with it even if its ugly.

Well, just my 2 cents...

Here's an example where copy by default does something surprising. I ran in to this when testing default values:
```
        sentinel = object()
        @dataclass
        class C:
            x: int = 0
            y: object = sentinel
        c = C()
        self.assertIs(c.y, sentinel)
```
This fails because c.y is a copy of sentinel, not sentinel itself.

@hynek: that really was a mega-thread! Thanks for the pointer.

From reading the thread, I think an optional well-known entry point like `__attrs_post_init__` does make the most sense. And do I understand correctly that it's left to the class authors to call `super().__attrs_post_init__` if they want to? That seems like a good decision. As the arguments to the post init function never change, you don't have the same problem as trying to call `__init__` through `super()`.

So, I propose that (subject to name changes) we have the generated `__init__` call `__dataclass_post_init__`, if one exists.

As far as extra parameters go: I'm not sure we can add it later, without changing the `__dataclass_post_init__` function signature, which again runs in to problems with `super()` calls.

The problem is you're using the JSON representation of ObjectId, more or less, to specify your matcher, rather than an actual ObjectId instance. This appears like it should work but it doesn't:
```
{"_id": {"$oid": "5a8f1e368f7936badfbb0cfa"}}
```
Instead, you want an actual ObjectId instance:
```
ObjectId('5a8f1e368f7936badfbb0cfa')
```
Now, for complicated reasons that are my fault, you need to import ObjectId from MockupDB instead of from PyMongo:
```
from mockupdb._bson import ObjectId as mockup_oid
```
Use that ObjectId in your matcher, and reply to the "find" command with a proper MongoDB "find" reply:
```
        request = self.server.receives(
            Command({"find": "dataSources", "filter": {"_id": mockup_oid('5a918f9fa08bff9c7688d3e1')}, "limit": 1, "singleBatch": True}, flags=4, namespace="app"))

    request.ok(cursor={'id': 0, 'firstBatch':[{'a': 2}]})
```
I'll fix MockupDB so you don't need the odd "import" hack to fix ObjectId comparisons.

Therefore, I see get_limits as more of a placeholder for further metrics such as the Maximum number of hostedzones.

I added a UploadWidgetMixin class to widgets.py that makes it possible to create your own widget:

```python
from django_file_form.widgets import UploadWidgetMixin
from django.forms import FileInput

class MyUploadWidget(UploadWidgetMixin, FileInput):
    pass
```

You can then use this in a form:

```python
class ExampleForm(BaseForm):
    input_file = UploadedFileField(widget=MyUploadWidget)
```

Thanks for you quick answer. I think a user cannot simply add devices to the entity registry manually, the actual component needs to support it. In my opinion the entity registry could supplement the `resolvenames` mode, without breaking existing setups. Just like the Hue component. I could think of this workflow:

1. Discover all Homematic devices by serial number (both in *regular* mode and *resolvenames* mode)

2. Check if the serial number (*unique id*) is present in the entity registry

   - If yes, then use that entity id for the device
   - If not, when *resolvenames* mode is active, try to resolve the name to an entity id and add it to the entity registry. This way we can skip name resolving for that particular device on future HASS restarts and we get stable entity ids.


This behavior would not break existing automations, as entity_ids would stay stable. In `resolvenames` mode the "dynamic" entity_ids would be migrated to the entity registry.

My fear with implementing constraints on the acquisition function optimization is that the algorithm might get stuck probing the same edge points. Since the Gaussian Process is not constrained it may yield optimal points outside the allowed bounds, leading the `acq_max` function to return the same edge point over and over and the GP not being updated (since no new knowledge is gained), in a never ending loop.

In my mind reparametrizing the target function (embedding the constraints in the new parametrization) seems like a more robust solution. However that can be hard or even impossible at times. Alternatively I could see a very clever choice or kernel also being a solution to this problem.

I know where the problem is. On Python 2 `sys.stdin` has to be compatible with with file object at C struct level in order to unicode literals be interpreted correctly. This C struct has its own encoding attribute, which is used in this case. The attribute is set to the right value, but if `win_unicode_console` is enabled in `sitecustomize` or `usercustomize`, the encoding is set to wrong value by `Py_Initialize` afterwards. To overcome this, my readline method resets the encoding back to the right value. The problem is that when `pyreadline` is active, it has its own readline which doesn't fix that encoding. A solution would be to detect pyreadline and wrap its hook with code fixing the encoding.

Here is a quick and dirty fixed `usercustomize`:

	import win_unicode_console
	import pyreadline.console as console
	import sys
	
	win_unicode_console.enable()
	
	original_install = console.install_readline
	
	def install_readline(hook):
		def hook_wrapper(*args, **kwargs):
			sys.stdin._reset_encoding()
			return hook(*args, **kwargs)
		
		original_install(hook_wrapper)
	
	console.install_readline = install_readline

Oh, I see what is the issue now. It looks like Documenter.jl “eats” the math delimiters. In your `src/index.md`, you have `\([0,1]\)`, but in the generated `build/index.md` it turns into `([0,1])` without the backslashes.

This happens before python-markdown-math even has a chance to process it, so this is not a my bug, but rather a bug in Documenter.jl. I have reported it as JuliaDocs/Documenter.jl#377.

As a workaround, you can use double backslashes, like `\\([0,1]\\)`.

At this point, the tests launch fine from eclipse, but the process hangs. I dug in and found out why: [here](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/13724653/pydev-pyunit-issue-when-using-thread-join-to-ensure-all-threads-are-joined). I don't like to modify odoo code, but I went ahead anyway. I made the following small modifications in `odoo/openerp/service/server.py`

Add this under `class ThreadedServer(CommonServer)`:
`initial_threads = []`

Then modify the condition in the `stop()` method of the same class so it looks like this:
`if thread != me and not thread.isDaemon() and thread.ident != self.main_thread_id and thread not in self.initial_threads:`

@AdamStelmaszczyk The changes look ok to me, but I still feel we are not addressing the main issue:

  - In `smarkets/streaming_api/ssession.py`, the `send_buffer` is still an immutable byte string instead of a bytearray. In particular, in `send()` we still need to make a copy of the previously buffered frames.

  - What I think needs to be done is to change the signature of `frame_encode` (or provide another function) that takes a frame and also a bytearray and appends the encoded frame to the given bytearray.

  - Then, in `send()` method, replace:
```python
    self.send_buffer += frame_encode(self.out_payload.SerializeToString())
```

by something like:

```python
    frame_encode(self.send_buffer, self.out_payload.SerializeToString())
```

  - Similarly, in `flush()`, instead of:

```python
    self.send_buffer = self.send_buffer[bytes_sent:]
```

use something like

```python
    self.send_buffer = bytearray(self.send_buffer[bytes_sent:])
# or
    self.send_buffer[0:] = self.send_buffer[bytes_sent:]

  - Similar for the couple of places where `self.send_buffer = b''` occurs

If a module includes a name in its `__all__`, then that provides a way for the module to signal to tools like pylint that the name will exist at runtime.

Pylint could continue to warn the project defining `__all__` that it's including a variable that doesn't exist, and that project can include a comment to disable pylint E0603 on that line if it wants. But the calling project is doing nothing wrong, and the information is available in the imported module's `__all__` to confirm that it is an intended variable name of the project (which, if it doesn't exist, is that project's fault, not the importer's).

That said, one thing I value very highly is backwards-compatibility. Sorry for breaking that code!

If we can extend the API a bit to make it more flexible, without breaking existing stuff and while still refactoring and keeping the code simple, then I would like to be able to handle your use case.

Do all of your objects share a common base class? Is there a way to enumerate them? I'm trying to think of what is the most common and best way to do this.

One way would be to have some global flag that disables features, e.g. disabling use of `__slots__`, disabling use of `__getnewargs_ex__`, etc.

But, disabling those features would be global across the entire encode operation, and I really don't like anything global (despite the globals that we do expose via the API) which is why I ask about the types of classes you're interesting in handling.  Reading the SO thread, it sounds like they all inherit from a common Message class?

That suggests that we might be able to have a table that disables features for specific classes and (optionally) their descendent classes. That'd at least make jp more flexible and capable of handling your use case.

Another crazy idea -- you could monkey-patch that Message class to kill the `__getstate__` method. It is python, afterall.  Yes, hacky, but that mght work.

The one thing that'll ensure that these features work for your use case is to whittle the use case down into a test case that we can add to the repo.  That way we'll never break your code because our tests will protect it. Do you think you might be able to help whittle this problem down to its bare essentials in the form of a test?

I thought I should mention I wrote a C#/.NET port of the Java (read-only) Annoy as part of my fork of Word2vec.Tools. It appears to work correctly but much too slowly, so it might not be useful in its current state. 

I haven't done performance tests, but it appears that running gensim within a Docker image is much faster, with or without using an index, meaning an indexed (less accurate) lookup is generally far slower than even an unindexed lookup in gensim. Partly this may be because I don't use a Memory Mapped File, but there are many other optimizations lacking (e.g. a lot of objects being created unnecessarily elsewhere in the supporting code).

It can only _read_ an annoy index, not create one (just like the Java version). However, unlike the Java port, it supports index files larger than 4 GB.

I haven't had time to work on it for a while so I thought I'd mention it in case anyone wanted to pick up the project or is looking for a starting point for a more proper C# port.

https://github.com/quole/Word2vec.Tools/blob/master/Word2vec.Tools/AnnoyIndex.cs

Also somewhat related: this [saga of improving Annoy baseline benchmark using BLAS](https://github.com/erikbern/ann-benchmarks/pull/5) (vectorization + parallelization).

Such optimizations had greater effect there than here (as brute force does more work) but it will be exciting to see this stuff in Annoy! Erik is pushing Annoy to ever more greatness :)

Here we can see a use-after-free bug. Looking at the code in [glTF2Exporter.cpp](https://github.com/Matter-and-Form/assimp/blob/feature/gltf2/code/glTF2Exporter.cpp#L279) the reason is that you're creating a temporary `std::string` but using its `.c_str()` after it's been destroyed. You need to keep the string alive if you need the value. The other variant of `GetMatTexProp` has the same bug.

Marking as a feature request. Unfortunately, there is not much that can be done to use the service with botocore as of now. In order to get this working, we would need a `service-2.json` file, which is a JSON model that represents the API for that service. Furthermore as mentioned in our [contributing guide](https://github.com/boto/botocore/blob/develop/CONTRIBUTING.rst#contributing-code), these models are generated upstream from botocore. So we would need to get the model from our upstream source in order to pull it into botocore and we do not accept pull requests for these models.

The best that you will be able to do for now is try to write a `service-2.json` by hand for the service. Then with this `service-2.json` you can run the AWS CLI [add-model](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/cli/latest/reference/configure/add-model.html) to start using a client for that model. However, there is no guarantee that you will be able to write a working `service-2.json` for the service and there really is not any documentation on how to understand/write these models.

If you want to find ways to interact stuff inside of the canvas element, you need to find some other way/library. This library relies on selenium and as far I know what you seek is not directly supported by selenium. Therefore it's not supported by us either.

By Googleling you can find some solutions how to interact with stuff inside of canvas with selenium. But those solution are purely application specific. I haven't tested anything with canvas and therefore cannot give a good answer, perhaps it would best to ask the same question from Robot Framework user group. @perrygeo as per discussion yesterday, I think `mapbox datasets action-resource` will be a fine pattern.

There's a bug in our Datasets API documention. It says

> Using the dataset API involves interacting with two types of resources: a dataset, which is a collection of features.

But there are actually *four* types of resources in `/datasets/v1/{account}/{dataset}/features/{feature}`:

1. The datasets container `/datasets/v1/{account}`.
2. The dataset `/datasets/v1/{account}/{dataset}`.
3. The dataset's feature container `/datasets/v1/{account}/{dataset}/features`.
4. The feature `/datasets/v1/{account}/{dataset}/features/{feature}`.

GETs on these 4 different URLs give 4 different responses. 4 different kinds of resources.

So, I propose we change your sketch above to 

```
mapbox datasets list
mapbox datasets create [args]

mapbox datasets read-dataset DSNAME [args]
mapbox datasets update-dataset DSNAME [args]
mapbox datasets delete-dataset DSNAME [args]

mapbox datasets list-features DSNAME [args]
mapbox datasets batch-update-features DSNAME [args]

mapbox datasets read-feature DSNAME FNAME [args]
mapbox datasets update-feature DSNAME FNAME [args]
mapbox datasets delete-feature DSNAME FNAME [args]
```

Worth combining the DSNAME and FNAME into one argument? Something like `DSNAME:FNAME`?

Hello Jaraco,

Finally getting around to replying to your comment from https://github.com/pypa/pip/issues/3989#issuecomment-249711001

I scanned above - and shall give as short a reply as possible.

While an OS filesystem may support names with spaces it does not make them suddenly better than names without spaces.
Wonderful that setuptools supports installations that want to use that feature. But forcing it on all is dogma. It becomes your opinion of what is best for all becoming the law.

In - what I would call 'UserSpace' these may be useful (e.g., what the python developer is developing as a User application) - but for normal 'binary' packaging (what platform packagers are doing to make a language such as python more easily available) they may be undesired - aka a hindrance for normal systems administration.

In short, you forcing packagers and system admins to support spaces in all cases - meaning they must review and/or rewrite maintenance 'scripts'. Leaving the solution as a task of an individual packager only promotes non-uniformity (at best). In my case I just stopped trying. If you follow the path - I first contacted python who said, not my problem, try talking with 'pip' - and pip said you are the real owner.

IMHO - if you want to support portability and 'freedom' have 'spaces' as a default, but also demonstrate an option to either replace with an underscore, a dash, or remove and FirstLetterCapitalize. Let the 'user' of setuptools decide what is best for them.

For now:
a) there is not a simple solution
b) the easiest hack I can come up with is scan for files with 'unfortunate' characters (for the installer template format) and rename them. End result is that it never looks as documented.
c) Or, just do not package at all. setuptools becomes the show-stopper rather than as an asset to packaging and installing.

IMHO - it is about choice. I hope you will consider supporting a choice here.

As for a "proper" solution, the problem is that data inside these tags is not JSON, it looks more like JavaScript, so likely it should be parsed with a JavaScript parser like https://github.com/rspivak/slimit, not with a JSON parser. 

But I recall these parsers are not always complete, and they can slow things down, so starting with just comment stripping using a regex sounds fine to me - unless there is a JS parser which is robust and fast enough.

There is an additional gotcha with a "proper" solution: HTML comments still have to be striped before passing to JS parser (see https://github.com/scrapy/parsel/issues/43).

Thanks for filing this, @ketralnis. I see what you're trying to do, and I agree it may be appropriate in some situations. `reset_offsets` doesn't actually touch ZooKeeper directly, it only issues an `OffsetRequest` and resets PyKafka's internal offset counters for the specified partitions. In cases where you're resetting internal offsets for partitions owned by the consumer you're operating on and you're using `auto_commit_enable=True`, this does result in an update to ZooKeeper's held offsets when the automatic commit occurs. This is because a PyKafka consumer doesn't actually have any way to affect the ZooKeeper offsets for partitions it doesn't own.

I can see potentially adding a `partition_offsets` argument to `commit_offsets` so that [this logic](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/e18bdeb3586fab21493b755d564d6b4aa8ec7ef2/pykafka/simpleconsumer.py#L453-L463) would not have to be limited only to `self._partitions`. I can't come up with anything that this would break off the top of my head, so if it's a pull request you'd like to pursue, I think it's reasonable. That said, this operation sounds like something I'd personally sooner look directly to ZooKeeper/kazoo for than PyKafka.

Unlike with a `SerialProcessor`, which does not catch any potential exception from `builder.update_targets`, letting the exception kill the process will only kill one out of many worker processes. I think the idea to completely halt the builder is fine: in that case, we *do* want to catch the exception in the runner/processor and then do what is needed to stop all workers.

I think you are confusing a few things. Custodian does not ALWAYS terminate a job. When it finds that an error can be corrected, it will modify parameters and resubmit. terminate_func is specified by the handlers, to deal with unrecoverable errors. 

As for **another** job being run, this is not a Custodian issue. It is a Fireworks issue. Custodian only deals with each job as a standalone thing. There is no workflow (other than internal error correction) built in. Pls contact @computron to solve it.

I'll keep playing the devil's advocate on this.

As a user of many packages but a developer on some, I'd much rather have hard breaks _with informative messages telling me what changed and how to fix it_ than a million deprecation warnings which will be ignored.  In the vast majority of cases the deprecations will keep being ignored; code will break now or later.  Our current _modus operandi_ is to warn people for a while and then do a hard break.  Again, I think this is worse from a user standpoint than an immediate break with instructions on why/how to fix it.

There are API changes where keeping the old way around is appropriate.  When we can't detect the change, or it involves moving or deleting an entire function for example.  However, when we occasionally merge in the wrong choices, like when kwargs have *_x or *_y instead of a single iterable, the last thing we want to do is sit on it for another year or two. 

Because when we delay, the docstring keeps teaching people to do it wrong.  Yes, deprecations are being thrown - and ignored because it works the other way, and that's what the docs say.  So we lead ever more people toward trouble when we pull the trigger on the change.

If it is possible to detect the old method of input and output an informative error message, I'd argue it is not only advisable but _always better_ to fix the API immediately in such cases.

Looks like there's an even bigger problem. Peak candidates are found with ```label```, and coordinates generated with ```measure.regionprops```. Currently this is a list, sorted leftmost peaks first. Peak candidates are assessed in this order so an early peak will suppress its neighbours even if the later peaks have higher intensities. This is particularly noticeable when low value peaks near -pi/2 suppress higher valued ones near +pi/2.

The solution is to sort the ```props``` list based on the maximum intensity of each label.

Hi @jamadden while trying to port this to python 3 we noticed that the combination of pure python Acquisition, AccessControll and RestrictedPython together breaks at this point.

The problem as best as I can describe it is, that there is no way to do a perfect proxy in python. In this case, that means that one cannot get at the `__dict__` of a class object in python 2.7, but always gets a `dict_proxy` instead, which then cannot be assigned as the `__dict__` of another instance.

So, can you please explain more about the use case in which this arose? We (at the Zope Resurrection Sprint in Halle) suspect that supporting `object.__getattribute__` may be something that does / should not happen outside of Zope itself - so can you name more use cases?

To be more specific, we think that also the pure python implementation of `persistant.Persistant` is probably not something we cannot support in the future (especially since there is a working C implementation that also works for python3).

So, can you please give some updates?

With fresh eyes, I have found the issue this morning. For interest, here is how to reproduce the problem I was having:

`import numexpr as ne
a = rnd.randn(1000, 32)
a = np.asfortranarray(a)  # I loaded a matrix from file, which (unknown to me) was Fortran contiguous
b = rnd.randn(103, 32)
d = ne.evaluate("(a - b) / ard")
print d.flags`

Giving:
`  C_CONTIGUOUS : False
  F_CONTIGUOUS : False`

This caused poor performance in numba code that followed. The root cause, however, was the Fortran contiguous array `a` (which in my code was loaded from disk). My incorrect understanding was that numexpr was outputting a weirdly ordered array for no reason and without a message, possibly causing problems with further numpy/numba code.

BTW, if you're wondering how the fmath can be just as accurate as std::exp, std::log but still be faster, I think it's mostly because they don't conform to the IEEE rules about nan's and inf's and such.  So for bad inputs, they probably do something different than what the IEEE rules dictate.  But in GalSim, for bad inputs, I don't think we care much whether the output is perfectly IEEE compliant.

The other advantage is that they can do 2 or 4 at once within the context of SSE commands, which obviously gives a big speed boost in those routines.

> is there (should there be?) a way to configure at compile time to use the fast math stuff or not?

The fmath expd is accurate at the 1.e-15 level.  Maybe that's one significant digit off from the std library version of exp, but I think always good enough for our purposes.  The single-precision versions are accurate to the full single precision, so that's not ever an issue.

below the split of the control flow

the main entry points are those from `Level 3`, being methods of `Experiment` class()
```
Level 1 dataframe generators:
 - results.delta_to_dataframe()
 - results.delta_to_dataframe_all_variants()
 - results.feature_check_to_dataframe()
 - results.early_stopping_to_dataframe()

Level 1 statistics workers:
 - statistics.delta()
 - early_stopping.group_sequential()        
 - early_stopping.bayes_factor()
 - early_stopping.bayes_precision()
 - statistics.chi_square()

Level 2 workers:
 - experiment._binned_deltas()              calls      statistics.delta()
                                            and        results.delta_to_dataframe_all_variants()

 - experiment._delta_all_variants()         calls      statistics.delta()
                                            and        results.delta_to_dataframe_all_variants()

 - experiment._feature_check_all_variants() calls      statistics.delta()
                                            and        statistics.chi_square()
                                            and        results.feature_check_to_dataframe()


Level 2.5 workers:
 - experiment.subgroups_deltas()            calls      experiment._binned_deltas()

 - experiment.time_dependent_deltas         calls      experiment._binned_deltas()


Level 3 workers / Experiment class methods:
 - Experiment.fixed_horizon_delta()         calls      experiment._delta_all_variants()

 - Experiment.group_sequential_delta()      calls      early_stopping.group_sequential()
                                            and        results.early_stopping_to_dataframe()

 - Experiment.bayes_factor_delta()          calls      early_stopping.bayes_factor()
                                            and        results.early_stopping_to_dataframe()

 - Experiment.bayes_precision()             calls      early_stopping.bayes_precision()
                                            and        results.early_stopping_to_dataframe()

 - Experiment.feature_check()               calls      experiment._feature_check_all_variants()

 - Experiment.sga()                         calls      experiment.subgroups_deltas()

 - Experiment.trend()                       calls      experiment.time_dependent_deltas()
```

> Here is some solution with patching existing jsonschema validator:
>
> from connexion.decorators.validation import RequestBodyValidatorimport jsonschemaimport six
>
> # via https://python-jsonschema.readthedocs.io/def extend_with_nullable_support(validator_class):
>     """Add support for null values in request body."""
>     validate_properties = validator_class.VALIDATORS['properties']
>
>     def nullable_support(validator, properties, instance, schema):
>         for property_, subschema in six.iteritems(properties):
>             if property_ in instance and \
>              instance[property_] is None and \
>              subschema.get('x-nullable') is True:
>                 instance.pop(property_)  # exclude from following validation
>
>         for error in validate_properties(
>                 validator, properties, instance, schema):
>             yield error
>
>     return jsonschema.validators.extend(
>         validator_class, {'properties': nullable_support})
>
>
> Draft4ValidatorSupportNullable = extend_with_nullable_support(
>     jsonschema.Draft4Validator)
>
> class RequestBodyValidatorSupportNullable(RequestBodyValidator):
>     def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):

>
> validator_map = {
>     'body': RequestBodyValidatorSupportNullable
> }
>

Good news, everyone!
The current version of `connexion` can already raise validation errors if any unexpected key is present.
Following from the example above, with an API so defined:

```
definitions:
  Customer:
    type: object
    additionalProperties: false
    properties:
      id:
        type: integer
        format: int64
```
Code that returns a Customer object so defined:
  `{'id': 42, 'password': 's3cure', 'name': 'charlie'}`

Yields:
`  validation error: Additional properties are not allowed (u'password', u'name' were unexpected)`

I'd update the docs, and close the bug :)

Well, `requirements.txt` and `setup.py`/`install_requires` are different things. Here's a nice article by Donald Stufft, who is one of the maintainers of pip: https://caremad.io/2013/07/setup-vs-requirement/. 

Basically, a `requirements.txt` file is usually used when you need to describe an entire environment, and need to reproduce that environment. For example, if you're making a Django site, you might `pip install` a bunch of libraries locally, then do `pip freeze > requirements.txt` and you can recreate your exact environment on the production server. You will usually see req.txt files using `==`.

You need `setup.py` when you're creating things like libraries, smaller pieces that other people will install into their environments. So `setup.py` dependencies basically never use `==`, just setting the lower and upper bounds of their dependency versions. Like you mentioned, `cython >= 0.23` is something that should be in the `install_requires` section of `setup.py`.

There is some overlap between `requirements.txt` and `setup.py` dependencies, which is why people are often confused, but this is the gist of it.

Now, on the subject of `setup_requires` vs `install_requires`. You need jinja and cython in `install_requires` because pyrobuf needs these libs to be importable during normal operation. When I type `pyrobuf` after having already successfully installed pyrobuf, the code in `pyrobuf.__main__:main` will want to import jinja2 and cython, so they need to be present. This is how normal dependencies work. A lot of libraries have `install_requires` dependencies, it's a very common thing. It's worth mentioning when libs are installed using pip, pip will fetch their `install_requires` dependencies and put them into the current environment.

Now, pyrobuf is using jinja2 and cython in `setup.py`. This is different. `setup.py` is executed when you're building your sdist (that's the .tar.gz file you upload to PyPI), but also when that package is being *installed* by your users*. And it's being run during installation, so you can't count on your normal dependencies (`install_requires` dependencies) being already installed, since they're probably being installed in parallel. So that's where `setup_requires` comes into play, here you tell setuptools what libraries you need to execute your `setup.py`. The libraries you list in `setup_requires` won't actually be present after the install is finished. (Hence my second commit ;) Now this is generally less common since most libraries don't actually need much (if any) logic in their `setup.py`, it's usually just metadata. I mentioned when you `pip install` a library, pip will also install all `install_requires` dependencies; this is in contrast to `setup_requires`. `Setup_requires` dependencies will actually be installed by setuptools using easy_install into a special directory. In some cases this can be important.

\* This is true for sdists, there is another format called wheel where there is no logic executed during installation, but wheels with C extensions are platform-specific, it gets complicated.

Meanwhile, if one does not want to rebuild DyNet, the workaround is to remove gradient information from the model file. The following script works for me in Python 3.6 for the latest format of DyNet models:

```python
f = open("YOUR MODEL FILE NAME")

while True:
  line = f.readline()
  if line == "":
    break
  parameters = line.split()
  if parameters[-1] == "FULL_GRAD":
    n = int(parameters[-2])
    parameters[-2] = str(n//2)
    parameters[-1] = "ZERO_GRAD"
    line = " ".join(parameters)
    print(line)
    line = f.readline()
    print(line, end="")
    line = f.readline() # skipping gradient line
  else:
    print(line, end="")
    line = f.readline()
    print(line, end="")
```

The [transaction package](https://github.com/zopefoundation/transaction/blob/master/docs/doom.rst) has a concept of a "doomed" transaction:  one which is in a hosed state, from which it can never be committed.  Having our code fail inside ``__exit__`` would be something like that with two sharp edges:

- Failing while committing is actually more reasonable:  the back-end might actually barf here.
- Failing while aborting is a real problem:  which exception should surface?

FWIW, the Zope transaction's "two-phase commit" tries to deal with the problems of the first, by breaking up the "commit" into slices, for distribution across multiple [resource managers](https://github.com/zopefoundation/transaction/blob/master/docs/resourcemanager.rst) (e.g., a database and a mail-sender).  For each of the following methods, the transaction calls each resource manager in turn):

- ``tpc_begin`` (can we commit?)
- ``commit``(do the work, but don't finalize)
- ``tpc_vote`` (were there problems?  last chance to back out)
- ``tpc_finish`` (finalize;  this must *never* fail:  if it does, we've lost consistency)
- ``tpc_abort`` (called if any RM raises from ``tpc_vote``:  must *never* fail).

Workaround for anyone on `1.0.0` is to use private `_properties` e.g.

`job_config._properties['labels']['mylabel'] = 'myvalue'`

The "obvious" fix would be to use a per-CI-run instance (using our normal `unique_id()` helper, which is what we did prior to #3951.  However, the Spanner folks throttle us at a maximum of 3 instances, and we have to keep one (`gcp-streaming-systests`) around, pre-populated.  That means that even if we mangle the instance name, and never screw up the cleanup, we can have *at most* two CI runs happening at one time.

We *could* try mangling the database names, rather than the instance name:  it is possible that we wouldn't hit a different quota limit for databases-within-an-instance.

There isn't a great way in `google-auth` of getting this in use, here is a super hacky approach.

```python
In [1]: import google.auth

In [2]: from openssl_signer import OpenSSLSigner

In [3]: 

In [3]: credentials, _ = google.auth.default()

In [4]: credentials
Out[4]: <google.oauth2.service_account.Credentials at 0x7fe1c8296780>

In [5]: old_signer = credentials._signer

In [6]: old_signer
Out[6]: <google.auth.crypt.RSASigner at 0x7fe1c82492e8>

In [7]: key = old_signer._key.save_pkcs1()

In [8]: key[:30]
Out[8]: b'-----BEGIN RSA PRIVATE KEY----'

In [9]: new_signer = OpenSSLSigner.from_string(
   ...:     key, key_id=old_signer.key_id)
   ...: 

In [10]: message = b'sanity check'

In [11]: old_signer.sign(message) == new_signer.sign(message)
Out[11]: True

In [12]: 

In [12]: credentials._signer = new_signer
```

actually comparing these two we see about a 46x speedup:

```python

In [15]: %timeit new_signer.sign(message)
712 µs ± 3.53 µs per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1000 loops each)

In [16]: %timeit new_signer.sign(message)

The `setup.cfg` option values are treated as bare strings, and as a result, your quoting hides the `-` from `ArgParse`. So, I repro given your example `setup.cfg` above, but solve via the following setup.cfg edit in the pex repo (no single-quotes around `-v` - also added a second arg):
```diff
$ git diff -U1 setup.cfg
diff --git a/setup.cfg b/setup.cfg
index 5e40900..8a490f0 100644
--- a/setup.cfg
+++ b/setup.cfg
@@ -2 +2,4 @@
 universal = 1
+
+[bdist_pex]
+pex-args = -v --python=python3
```

Which finds success:
```
$ python2.7 setup.py bdist_pex
running bdist_pex
pex: Resolving distributions: 2309.4ms       
pex:     Packaging pex: 503.1ms
  pex 1.1.10
  setuptools 20.10.1
Writing pex to /home/jsirois/dev/pantsbuild/jsirois-pex/dist/pex-1.1.10.pex
$ head -1 dist/pex-1.1.10.pex 
#!/usr/bin/env python3.5
```

Hi Benjamin. I've updated the analysis document commit with your new experimental results for edge cases and force-pushed it on feature/bookmarks. There are a couple open questions I've added which you might want to look into. I don't think any of them are show-stoppers, but the "bookmarks must be unique across all stories" question may cause us some headaches.

I'll move on to seeing how much of the acceptance tests I can get merged and let you know where I end up.

After that, I suggest you open a new pull request (PR) using the new `feature/bookmarks` branch as the base. Before that you should rebase your own `feature/bookmarks` such that it builds upon what's already been merged. That's how we'll proceed until all commits are merged. So your branch will progressively contain fewer and fewer commits as the prior ones are merged.

We should also strive to keep the PR (commentary) length more manageable. This one has gotten too long to comfortably navigate, partly because of so many extended code insertions. Perhaps better if we use github links for those, like this one:  
https://github.com/python-openxml/python-docx/blob/d09fcf42ed3d2aef1f2d0fa347ac918f41566227/docx/blkcntnr.py#L27-L39  

The technique is described here:  
https://blog.mariusschulz.com/2015/07/25/sharing-line-highlights-in-github-files

I'll leave another comment when I've merged what I can of the acceptance tests.

@spohner - I think you can pick this up more or less from the beginning of implementation. The analysis document (in the first commit above) is fairly well developed, so should provide the background needed. @tanyunshi also provided a "spiked" implementation, so I'm sure that's useful background as well, but to proceed with a merge we'll need tests, and it will need to be in test-driven commits.

The first step would be developing the acceptance test(s) in (`features/{something}.feature`). Then next is the unit-test/code pair that addresses the first error in the acceptance test, and so on in rinse-repeat style until the acceptance test passes. You can look at a couple of the recent pull requests for some idea how it all goes:

These two are pretty good examples:
https://github.com/python-openxml/python-docx/pull/261
https://github.com/python-openxml/python-docx/pull/291

Merges maintain a strictly test-driven discipline; it's the only way to keep the library robust while keeping maintenance/committer effort within doable levels :)  The basic idea is:

* No code gets merged without its corresponding unit test.
* No unit test gets written without a failing acceptance test.
* Each commit is one atomic piece of functionality, generally a single property or method.

Let me know with any questions once you've had a chance to look through :)

@christianmemije I think with this simplified approach, there is no need to include a facility selector at all in the login UI.

To paraphrase Jamie, 99% of the time we won't need to inquire what facility the user is logging into. Problems only come up when there are collisions in both username and passwords. For example, when a username + password set belong to 2+ facilities, or when a learner's username belongs in 2+ facilities. 

If such ambiguities can be detected after login info input, after pressing sign in, a modal can pop up prompting the user to clarify what facility they actually want to go to. This way we reduce UI clutter.

So the issue is that @navakk9 has three pythons: The 2.7 from the python website, 3.5 from homebrew, and 2.7 that's built in to the Mac (located at `/usr/bin/python`). Running `python kolibri.pex` or `python3 kolibri.pex` works, because it uses the first two, which seems to be doing the right thing and adding the`six` library we want.

The issue is when we do `./kolibri.pex`, which calls the builtin python located at `/usr/bin/python`. Instead of using the `six` module bundled in `django.dist`, it's using another `six` module found in `/Library`.

Basically, it boils down to incompatible libraries. The built-in python may have additional safeguards and restrictions regarding path modification that's making us use an old version of `six`. The fix here might be to add `six` into BBQ itself (six is implemented in just one file), then have BBQ use that directly, instead of depending on an installed vesrion.

Hi @benjaoming @ralphiee22 I wrote some [code](https://github.com/learningequality/kolibri/compare/release-v0.7.x...lyw07:move_future_modules) to fix this issue and https://github.com/learningequality/iceqube/issues/31. It moves the directory of those python 2 modules, for example `html` from `future` and `concurrent` from `futures` to a subdirectory called `py2only` inside `kolibri/dist`. It also adds the directory of `py2only` to the PYTHONPATH only when running in Python 2. 

So when it's in Python 2, the PYTHONPATH will be something like
`["kolibri/dist", <other paths>, "kolibri/dist/py2only"]`
to make those python 2 modules available.

When it's in Python 3, the PYTHONPATH will be
`["kolibri/dist", <other paths>]`
to make those python 2 modules unavailable so that kolibri will use the Python 3 built-in modules.

I tested on OS X with the pex file and on Ubuntu 14.04/16.04/17.04/17.10 with the whl file under both Python 2 and Python 3. They all worked without both issues we have for `future` and `futures`. 

However, I'm not sure if this is a good approach and it's worth it to create a PR for this, since I feel like this code has some weird design and seems a bit hard-coded. But this is the way I can think of to fix both issues currently. When you have time, could you please take a look at the code and tell me about your thoughts? If you think there are other ways to fix those issues, please let me know :) Thank you!

After digging a bit further, I don't think this is an AllenNLP bug at all. It seems like a problem with pytorch. It happens only when moving the model onto GPU, e.g.

```python
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

features = 50
hidden_size = 50
num_layers = 2
cuda_device = 1    # on a machine with multiple GPUs (>0)

model = nn.Module()
model.rnn = nn.RNN(input_size=features, hidden_size=hidden_size, num_layers=num_layers)
model.cuda(cuda_device)
```

After model.cuda(cuda_device), there will be memory used on the specified GPU (in this case, cuda_device=1), as well as the default GPU (which is usually device 0).

```python
seq_len = 10
batch_size = 32

X_train = torch.randn(seq_len, batch_size, features)
X_train = Variable(X_train).cuda(cuda_device)
```

The same problem doesn't seem to occur when moving tensors onto GPU (as above).

We could try to make sure this doesn't happen by adding a with construct around the model.cuda line in AllenNLP, i.e.:

```python
with torch.cuda.device(cuda_device):
    model.cuda(cuda_device)
```

...but I think the chance of this causing problems for other people is pretty low, so it may not be worth making a ton of effort here. And maybe the issue will be addressed in pytorch in the future.

Thanks for the code snippet!   If we added that to the library we could maintain the current format, and reference it as the supported method to read it the embeddings without the original raw text file.

Removing spaces happens here: https://github.com/allenai/allennlp/blob/152b590caab8732792689b85686788f2197b93c4/allennlp/data/tokenizers/word_splitter.py#L129-L153

You could imagine raising a warning if the returned list becomes empty, though that warning could be annoying if you're already handling this in your code (which I think is the right thing to do).

But for the original problem, you'd only get this error if _none_ of the instances in your batch had any tokens in it, right?  Because we already handle the case where there's some padding, and it's fine.  This is a problem because none of the `TextFields` had `num_token_characters`, so it doesn't show up in the `padding_lengths` that get passed to the `TokenIndexer`, and things crash.

Would it be enough to give a better error message than a `KeyError` here?  Basically, catch the `KeyError` and log a fatal error saying that you provided a batch that looks like it's all padding, and there's probably a data processing error in your code?

Further investigation here has revealed a few things:

1. If you quit phantomjs and then try to quickly instantiate a new one, you're looking for pain. There's a 30s timer in the code that in theory makes this possible, since the old phantomjs doesn't go away immediately. But if 30s elapse and your old one is still around, the new one can't be created and then crashes your program. Why it's like this, I know not, but it's certainly annoying. It crashes with:

        Traceback (most recent call last):
          File "/var/www/court-listener/alert/scrapers/management/commands/cl_scrape_opinions.py", line 430, in handle
            self.parse_and_scrape_site(mod, full_crawl)
          File "/var/www/court-listener/alert/scrapers/management/commands/cl_back_scrape_opinions.py", line 11, in parse_and_scrape_site
            for site in site_yielder(mod.Site().back_scrape_iterable, mod):
          File "/usr/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/juriscraper/lib/importer.py", line 51, in site_yielder
            site._download_backwards(i)
          File "/usr/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/juriscraper/opinions/united_states/state/tex.py", line 237, in _download_backwards
            self.html = self._download()
          File "/usr/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/juriscraper/opinions/united_states/state/tex.py", line 56, in _download
            service_log_path=os.path.devnull,  # Disable ghostdriver.log
          File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/selenium/webdriver/phantomjs/webdriver.py", line 50, in __init__
            self.service.start()
          File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/selenium/webdriver/phantomjs/service.py", line 69, in start
            raise WebDriverException("Can not connect to GhostDriver")
        WebDriverException: Message: 'Can not connect to GhostDriver'

2. The way we currently import modules means that there's no long-living object that persists between scrapes of one site and the next (or multiple scrapes of the same site, in the case of a backscraper). This means that we need to either load PhantomJS in the caller and pass it to the scraper (which sucks), or change Juriscraper's caller API. Current code works like this:

        module_strings = build_module_list(court_id)
        package, module = module_strings[i].rsplit('.', 1)
        mod = __import__("%s.%s" % (package, module),
                         globals(),
                         locals(),
                         [module])
        site = mod.Site()
        site.parse()

    A better approach would probably be to instantiate a new Juriscraper object that holds onto long-running stuff and does the wack-a-doo string conversion for you. Something like:

        j = Juriscraper()
        j.start()
        s = j.parse('tex')  # or j.parse('united_states.opinions'), or whatever.

    It's a much nicer API that moves the craziness out of the callers. What's more, it gives us a place where we can keep PhantomJS.

I've had a proper look into this today, and it turns out there's actually a few issues leading to it:

1. When a user hits Ctrl+C in the terminal, the [SIGINT signal is sent to the foreground process group](http://pubs.opengroup.org/onlinepubs/000095399/basedefs/xbd_chap11.html#tag_11_01_09), which means both the networking and the database processes get interrupted at the same time and not in sequence (first networking, then database) as the code in [`trinity.main()` would like](https://github.com/ethereum/py-evm/blob/master/trinity/main.py#L170-L175)
2. Our [database server catches the KeyboardInterrupt raised by the SIGINT](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/3.6/Lib/multiprocessing/managers.py#L147-L161) shuts down and exits silently, so we don't realize but it will have already terminated by the time we run `kill_process_gracefully(database_server_process)` in `trinity.main()`
3. Thanks to the above and #665, we get the `BrokenPipeError` in the chain syncer
4. Finally, the unhandled `KeyboardInterrupt`s seem to be caused by us using the `spawn` context for the multiprocessing package. Here are the relevant passages from https://docs.python.org/3.6/library/multiprocessing.html: 
 ```
On Unix using the spawn or forkserver start methods will also start a semaphore tracker process which tracks the unlinked named semaphores created by processes of the program.
[...]
If the SIGINT signal generated by Ctrl-C arrives while the main thread is blocked by a call to BoundedSemaphore.acquire(), Lock.acquire(), RLock.acquire(), Semaphore.acquire(), Condition.acquire() or Condition.wait() then the call will be immediately interrupted and KeyboardInterrupt will be raised.
 ```

Right now, it is only the `main` process that listens for this event and then calls `kill_trinity_gracefully` which is the same takedown routine we already have for when a user presses `CTRL+C`.

So, I believe we do actually have the same behavior already if you press `CTRL+C` right in that moment similar to when the `TxPool` calls `shutdown_host()` on the plugin context.

What happens right now (I believe) is that `kill_trinity_gracefully` is called *before* the networking process is setup but after the db process already started. Then, inside `kill_trinity_gracefully` we start of taking down the db process and shortly after the networking process is starting trying to connect to the db. So that should theoretically be exploitable right now if you `CTRL+C` at the right moment in time.

>this gets a little more complicated with multiple processes, but doable: the db process could track which processes are listening to it, and only shut down after it hears a termination event from every listening process

Yep, totally doable and probably worth following up on especially if we want isolated plugins to become popular and hence have a shitload of processes to manage (which btw, a modern browser also does, so I'm not worried about that).

However, I'd appreciate if we could push that into another issue. I can write down a quick issue and link to this thread.

This version of `_make_labels()` (inspired by http://stackoverflow.com/a/13837045/1441112) works for both:

```python
def _make_labels(nodes, links, vgen):
    eq_edges = defaultdict(set)
    for l in links:
        if l.post == EQ_POST:
            eq_edges[l.start].add(l.end)
            eq_edges[l.end].add(l.start)
    # thanks: http://stackoverflow.com/a/13837045/1441112
    seen = set()
    def conjunction(nodeid):
        nids = {nodeid}
        while nids:
            nid = nids.pop()
            seen.add(nid)
            nids |= eq_edges[nid] - seen
            yield nid
    labels = {}
    # be sure to do LTOP_NODEID first so it gets h0
    for nid in chain([LTOP_NODEID], (node.nodeid for node in nodes)):
        if nid not in seen:
            lbl = vgen.new(HANDLESORT)[0]
            for conj_nid in conjunction(nid):
                labels[conj_nid] = lbl
    return labels
```

[`source`](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Source_(command)) is a bash built-in. You must "source" rather than subprocess when you want the script to affect the context of the current process.  To make `conda activate` work, `conda` actually needs to become a shell function rather than an executable.  The `conda` command is loaded as a shell function in conda 4.4 and 4.5 when you execute something like

    source /home/USERNAME/anaconda2/etc/profile.d/conda.sh

from above.  Compare the output of `type -a conda` before and after issuing that command.  This is all complicated by how shells initialize themselves differently in "interactive" vs "non-interactive" mode.

We're trying to clean this up one step further in conda 4.6.  The recommended way to initialize conda in a bash shell where `~/.bashrc` or `~/.bash_profile` is not sourced at shell startup will be

    eval "$(/home/USERNAME/anaconda2/bin/conda shell.bash hook)"

which largely follows the same pattern established by the excellent [`direnv`](https://direnv.net/) project.

I think the best solution would be to have conda generate a new-style `.dist-info` folder in case they are not created by default by `python setup.py install` which is likely to be the case in most cases. 

This might be useful (pip is using a vendored copy of it).

http://distlib.readthedocs.org/en/latest/

Or alternatively, to convert an egg into new-style dist-info metadata:

https://bitbucket.org/pypa/wheel/src/bdf053a70200c5857c250c2044a2d91da23db4a9/wheel/egg2wheel.py?at=default#cl-17

Now I understand that this can be a lot of work for little benefit for pure-conda users. Still the fact that dist-info has now been promoted has a static metadata format specifically devised to be installer / distribution agnostic makes me think that conda should try its best to generate those metadata as well for general interoperability purpose.

It's unfortunate that only the `wheel` package and tooling support it at the moment.

I get that, @jjhelmus, but your recipe *doesn't ask for libgdal*. It only asks for `gdal`, and leaves it to the solver to figure out which version of libgdal to pick up. 

There are multiple conflicting concerns here when designing the dependency solver's goals. In the case of `libgdal` for instance, you're not just picking up an older version of `libgdal`, but also older versions of other packages as well. The "downgrade" score for build 0 of `libgdal` is actually lower: by downgrading `libgdal` only 2 builds, I'm downgrading other packages less.

At some point you have to throw up your hands and say _the metadata just has to be right_. In this case I would argue the best solution is a version bump for `libgdal` and stronger version specs for `gdal`.
