@wulmer thanks!  Much appreciated.  Some setup steps here, but this may not be complete, just due to the not-quite-always-reproducible nature of build environments (I may have something installed that I've forgotten to list):

- tests of bash assume that bash is somewhere on PATH.  This probably means that "git" needs to be a test-time requirement of this package, at least on windows, which packages "Git for Windows" which includes bash.  What this means for you is that you'll need to install some form of bash somewhere on PATH that stays on PATH through the test process (anywhere not in the conda root env should be fine).  This PR is meant to support bash generally on Windows, so I'm curious to test the different ways of getting it: cygwin, MSYS, MSYS2 - please add any others you're aware of, so that we minimize blind spots.

- Testing conda almost always ruins my miniconda install at some point.  I recommend a dedicated miniconda install, without adding it to PATH (or, to make life easier, a different user or a clean VM and add it to PATH).  If you don't add the installation, to PATH, make sure all of your exe calls use the full path to the exe, just to avoid confusion.  From there, ```conda install setuptools pytest``` (because they is missing otherwise), then remove the conda folder and conda egg-info from site-packages.  Finally, ```<full path to dedicated miniconda python> setup.py develop``` in your git checkout.  Note that ```develop``` doesn't keep shell scripts up to date - only Python files.  You may need to re-run it, or re-run ```<python> setup.py install``` if ```develop``` is not copying shell scripts appropriately (but this might be a good bug to fix, so please report it or fix and PR)

- Run tests with full path to py.test.exe.  Mine is something like ``` c:\Miniconda2\Scripts\py.test.exe Z:\Documents\code\conda\tests\test_activate.py``` - z: is my network drive out of my VM.  Adjust accordingly for your system.

- The cmd.exe test suite runs OK, I believe.  The bash test suite on windows is about 2/3 there.  I have not yet tried this stuff on Linux or Mac.

Finally, I would like to move towards supporting different shells as simply config files, rather than things being hard-coded into Conda, for the sake of modularity and ease of adding other shells.  There is a glimpse of this in the test_activate file, where the shell collection is now a dictionary.  This is something I'm saving for a future PR, so that this PR doesn't get delayed any longer.  I welcome your thoughts and comments, though, especially if anything in this PR can make that future work easier.

This PR doesn't really introduce `CONDA_INSTALL_PREFIX`. The value is set up as a variable* however the intention is for setuptools to perform a post-install that steps through and replaces all `CONDA_INSTALL_PREFIX` values with the appropriate installation prefix.

If you install this PR via `python setup.py [install|develop]` then all of the `CONDA_INSTALL_PREFIX` values will vanish. The current issue is that I have yet to find a good way to do a post install/copy process when conda has been built/packaged as a dist.

*In theory this variable will allow you to use the conda package without installing it and simply configuring the `CONDA_INSTALL_PREFIX`. In practice this shouldn't be done, this should only be considered for simplifying the unittesting.

Thresholds (filter) solves part of that. Perhaps a multiplicative or otherwise non-linear combination model would help as well?

 E.g. 

combined_AR_comp = score_var_A * score_var_B / normfactor

Or something like 

gated_AR_comp = sigmoid[ score_var_B > 10] * score_var_A + sigmoid[ score_var_A > 10 ] * score_var_B

where sigmoid[x>N] is some appropriate nonlinear function, gating to a high value for x > N and a near-zero or perhaps even a negative for x < N.

Hi @andral,

You are right that RedHat has made some changes to how certificates are handled in the python 2.7.5 they ship. They have had the ability to enable https certificate verification since RHEL7.2, but it was disabled by default. In RHEL 7.4 they changed the default behavior for certs to be enabled, hence your breakage.

You can disable the default certificate verification by editing the cert-verification.cfg file in RedHat or by setting the PYTHONHTTPSVERIFY environment variable to 0.

More information/details here: https://access.redhat.com/articles/2039753#red-hat-enterprise-linux-7-resolution-4

Please try one of these options out and let me know if it resolves your issue.

Thanks,
Matt

It looks like this will be addressed by Curator, but not directly by enabling or disabling this feature.

From this [post](https://www.elastic.co/blog/elasticsearch-storage-the-true-story-2.0):

>Alternatively, we expect many users will want to utilize a “hot/warm” architecture using the shard allocation filtering feature. In this scenario, time-based indexes on hot nodes can be configured to be created with the default LZ4 compression; when they’re migrated to the warm nodes (configured with index.codec: best_compression in elasticsearch.yml), the indexes can be compressed by optimizing that index. It may be preferable to pay the CPU penalty of compression during this optimize process (which we often recommend executing when the cluster is known to be less utilized) than at the time of initial indexing.

With this being the case, it seems that Curator's existing shard allocation functionality will address this need.

The version in the meta information exists since the 5.0 release. But all 5.x releases just load one template with the name `metricbeat`. So only people starting with the 6.x releases have multiple templates and the version in the name. Someone running Metricbeat 6.0 with Elasticsearch 5.x will also have multiple templates.

So this is a problem that is only starting to happen. I'm hoping if we can get such a feature into curator that it's rather generic as people also modify templates and index patterns sometimes.
Also, please don't forget link to boto3 [Credentials](http://boto3.readthedocs.io/en/latest/guide/configuration.html) manual page in documentation for all possible ways of setting credentials.

That's the syntax for a _very_ old version of Curator (2.x, I think).  The 3.x syntax was different from that, and the 4.x syntax uses YAML configuration files.

If you want command-line syntax for Curator, you will need to do `pip install -U elasticsearch-curator==3.5.1` to go _back_ to that version.

After that, you'll need to run:

```
curator --host localhost delete indices --timestring '%Y.%m.%d' --older-than 10 --time-unit days
```

See: https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/client/curator/3.5/time-unit.html

Other than that, the documentation is all online.  Please refer to current documentation instead of older, unreliable information on blog posts.  The documentation for the most recent versions is at https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/client/curator/index.html

This article shows you how to create a streaming locator for your asset: https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/azure/jj129578.aspx#create_origin_URL_for_streaming. To reference a Smooth Streaming origin URL in a streaming media player, you must append the Path property with the name of the Smooth Streaming manifest file, followed by "/manifest". For example, using the origin URL shown in the previous example, the actual URL used by smooth streaming clients would be http://media.windows.net/52034bf6-dfae-4d83-aad3-3bd87dcb1a5d/samplevid.ism/manifest. And for HLS clients, the example would be http://media.windows.net/52034bf6-dfae-4d83-aad3-3bd87dcb1a5d/samplevid.isml/manifest(format=m3u8-aapl).

Meanwhile, your video file needs to be encoded as a multi-bitrates Mp4, if you want to have your asset dynamically packed on the fly to multiple streaming protocols. You will need 1 streaming reserved unit for this.

Hi @charliejllewellyn 

Note that the Storage repo (for issues) is here (the Storage team supports the client themselves, not the central SDK team where I am):
https://github.com/Azure/azure-storage-python

But I have context on this one, so let me give you what I know.

As ARM, Storage has constraints in term of ApiVersion for AzureStack support. Recommended ApiVersion for Azure Stack and Storage Data Plane is 2015-04-05. Being that ApiVersion is hardcoded into Storage Data client, this means you need to downgrade your SDK to [0.30.0](https://github.com/Azure/azure-storage-python/blob/master/ChangeLog.md#version-0300), which is the latest to support this version. As far as I know, this was the Storage team solution for this problem. But since I'm not part of this team, **I strongly encourage you to open an issue on their tracker, asking for Azure Stack story**.

For CLI, since is supports changing profile on the fly, downgrading package was not an option. We then decided to ship a new package as a mix-up of different ApiVersion/SDK version:
https://github.com/Azure/azure-multiapi-storage-python

This is **NOT** an official package, and official solution is the one from the Storage team. I cannot guarantee stability on this package, but this is how we solved this for Azure Storage + CLI + Stack.

Thanks for the contribution, but I can only accept the patch if it addresses the following:
1) patches should not include any whitespace/tab/space changes
2) Bullet is only C++2003, so no 'default' keyboard
3) it breaks the build (see travis/appveyor result)
4) Bullet3 is experimental and if you want to add double-precision build support, is requires modifying all OpenCL kernels so it works in single/double precision on GPU/OpenCL as well.
5) don't mix unrelated fixes in a single patch
If you are willing to address above, please re-open a new request.

> I don't see a mechanism for getting the files from the kernel to the webserver, only communicating paths, which require the filesystem to be the same.

This is why I apologized in my first response, "path" really should be "name".  What's being communicated in the message to the kernel are "names" & hashes of corresponding contents.  And the other way is "names" and actual file contents.  How the file contents make there way over the line is up for discussion, but I was thinking binary messages of some sort.

> It also doesn't solve that problem, it just punts it to the kernel. How does the package communicate this information to the kernel, such that the kernel knows at startup, before any imports, what resources are available?

Punting the problem to the kernel is the whole point.  Python has a mechanism for this, entry points, which means it's solved for IPython and Jupyter which is all I'm concerned about.  The generic messages allow other kernel authors to solve the problem how they want.  i.e. IJulia will have to implement their own registry, but as long as they implement the messages, they can do it however they want.

> If we use setuptools entrypoints for this, and communicate files from the kernel to the server at startup and only at startup, this means potentially 100s of MB of file transfer on every kernel startup to the web server. e.g. if a kernel plugin makes MathJax available, there's no mechanism to make the pieces available on request, which proxying http would do, instead it requires all possible resources to be moved at once to the server on every kernel start.

The caches stored in the web server would be persisted to the disk. On request, if a resource doesn't exist because blue message #2 hasn't been received yet, the request will be deferred until that message has been received.  Once the message is received, if the content still doesn't exist, 404, otherwise respond with the contents.

In order to have your extension working you need something like:
```
def _jupyter_nbextension_paths():
    return [{
            'section': "notebook",
            'src': os.path.join("static"),
            'dest': "my_extension/",
            'require' "my_extension/main")
    ]
```
The key `dest` is the destination and the value has a `/` at the end of the string, [1] will join the path, but the resulting path will also have a `/` at the end of the string. [2] will fail to create a symlink because it will consider that you want to create a symlink inside the folder `full_dest` which at this point does not exist.
`normpath` will normalize the `full_dest` string and will remove the `/` at the end. This will avoid the failure and will work fine.

[1] https://github.com/ellisonbg/notebook/blob/nbext-pain/notebook/nbextensions.py#L183
[2] https://github.com/ellisonbg/notebook/blob/nbext-pain/notebook/nbextensions.py#L197

The last bit is the problem. You don't put packages themselves on PYTHONPATH, you put the directories that contain them, i.e. `site-packages`. And most of the time, it's not necessary to set it at all.

I think we're in a pretty good place right now, in the sense that the method footprint on `Model` is still fairly lean.

Future additions can be decided on a case-by-case basis IMO. If it's functionality that makes sense for model instances only, I wouldn't want to rule out the inclusion of new methods if they can be named safely.

For general-purpose transformations, providing only the functional API is indeed a good solution. For example, if `flatten()` was added today, I doubt anyone would insist on a `Model.flatten()` method. Besides, such features can always be bundled into mixins for those who prefer the method API.

Currently, there are only a few core methods: `validate()` and `import_data()` are fundamental, and `get()` is pretty important now that fields may be unset. `convert()` is a strange one in that it doesn't touch the instance at all; it's essentially equivalent to `partial(transforms.convert, self.__class__)`.

`to_native()` and `to_primitive()` won't be causing any name clashes. The useless methods `iter()`, `flatten()`, and `from_flat()` have been axed.

So, the remaining question is what to do with the mapping-like methods. There are several options:

* Keep all of `keys()`, `values()`, and `items()` (and protect the names).
* Keep only `keys()` in order to make `dict(obj) == dict(obj._data)` an invariant.
* Move all of them out (into a mixin or behind a config option).

The `name` configuration scheme I presented earlier significantly reduces the annoyance factor of protecting reserved attributes. If no one's opposed to that plan, then preserving at least `keys()` starts to look attractive.

> Currently, the way parse_args is set up in setuptools_wrap.py, the only way to set the build type is by 
> setting it on the command line in the dutils section of the arguments (being the first one before any --), 

Indeed, this corresponds to the `--build-type` argument added by scikit-build to the setup.py global options. 

### Single-config vs multi-config generator

> and if not set in that place specifically, both cmake and make will be set with Release as their build 
> type. I am not sure of the scope of this issue, but is this intended behavior?

Defaulting to `Release` is indeed the expected behavior.

That said the current implementation unconditionally specify `-DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE:STRING=` at configure time and also `--config` when invoking cmake to build the project using the syntax `cmake --build /build/dir --config <config>`, it should be one or the other base on the type of generator.

In that regards, there are roughly two types of CMake generators:
* multi-config: Visual Studio, ....
* single-config:  Ninja, Make, Nmake, ... 

The key difference is that for multi-config generator, the build type is only known at built time whereas for single configuration generator it is a configure time parameter.

### Precedence of arguments

Arguments can be set in few different ways:
* command line
* keyword value passed to the setup function:
  * directly in `setup.py`
  * using `setup.cfg` (this is currently not tested)
* default values hard-coded in `setuptools_wrap.setup`

(In the future, we should also discuss how such value could be set in `pyproject.toml`)

Generally speaking value on the left take precedence on the one on the right : `command line` | `kewords` | `defaults`

This means that if a keyword is passed as `setup(..., foo=1)`, passing the command line argument `--foo 2` will lead to `foo` having the value `2`.

### The case of command line arguments

#### Description of the "type" of arguments

Now, as you described above, argument targeting different tools can be passed through the command line.

Generally speaking, within scikit-build we have:

```
/path/to/build-frontend  [build-frontend-args, ...]  [-- [build-backend-configure-args, ...] [-- [build-backend-buildtool-args]]]
```

which translate to this in the context of `setup.py`:

```
setup.py [setup-args|skbuild-args, ...] [-- [cmake-args, ...] [-- [build-tool-args, ...]]]
```

or more specifically (copied from [here](https://scikit-build.readthedocs.io/en/latest/usage.html#command-line-options)):

```
setup.py [global_opts] cmd1 [cmd1_opts] [cmd2 [cmd2_opts] ...] [skbuild_opts] [-- [cmake_opts] [-- [build_tool_opts]]]
```


#### The case of `--config` argument

In the current implementation of scikit-build, `--config` is a `skbuild_opts`

After being parsed by [create_skbuild_argparser](https://github.com/scikit-build/scikit-build/blob/3c6c2d162bf1182b8802fb2574e176427c893db2/skbuild/setuptools_wrap.py#L51-L68), the following happen with the corresponding value:

(1) Update of `cmake_args`
https://github.com/scikit-build/scikit-build/blob/3c6c2d162bf1182b8802fb2574e176427c893db2/skbuild/setuptools_wrap.py#L81

(2) Update of `build_tool_args`
https://github.com/scikit-build/scikit-build/blob/3c6c2d162bf1182b8802fb2574e176427c893db2/skbuild/setuptools_wrap.py#L86

(3) calling of `CMaker.make` with `make_args` (which is the same as `build_tool_args` and should be renamed for consistency)

https://github.com/scikit-build/scikit-build/blob/3c6c2d162bf1182b8802fb2574e176427c893db2/skbuild/setuptools_wrap.py#L537


(4) removal of `--config` from the final list of build tool arguments


https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0517/#config-settings

That said, it is worth noting that [PEP 517](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0517/#id4) doesn't support this model.

And I agree that `charms.reactive` is hard to grasp for newcomers, and triggers won't change that. I think that that is caused by the fact that we don't follow any conventional theory (such as state machines). Newcomers don't intuitively understand the reactive framework gives them because they have never seen anything like it. It's like trying to teach rust to someone who has only used c-like languages.

`charms.reactive` 2.0 addresses some of the "inconsistency" issues, the stuff that newcomers are most surprised of, but it still doesn't build on a conventional theory, so it will still be hard for newcomers to understand. Discussions like these are great to try to see if there isn't any existing theory we can base `charms.reactive` 2.0 on..

I think `charms.reactive` resembles a rule engine the most..

The pattern that I've adopted, and that I've seen several other charm authors use, is to eschew `@hook('install')` in favor of `@when_not('my_charm.installed')`.  I think that makes it clear what the real condition is (something is not installed that needs to be, regardless of what life-cycle event we're responding to), and avoids the issue you mentioned.

In general, I think `@hook` should be avoided at all costs.  The only place I use it is in interface layer classes, and because of that those suffer from issues with upgrades and debugging.  I'm really thinking that the framework needs to provide a synthetic state for connected relations so that we can deprecate `@hook` entirely.

Alright, I've figured it out.

`RealtimeSignalProcessor` is called by `post_save` database signal of the model. Thus, when we save `Product` model it's being updated in the search index.

When we save product in the dashboard, we firstly save product form and after that - all formsets, including category formset. Thus, categories being saved _after_ product and at the moment of the `RealtimeSignalProcessor` call product does not have categories assigned, which is basically the reason of product not being displayed on the category products list.

In order to get product updated in the search index within all related fields, you can trigger search index record update in `ProductUpdate.forms_valid` method. Oscar lets to make decision how to handle search index update by the user and does not integrate or implement signals to invalidate search index records.

In the future we intend to implement custom Elasticsearch wrapper, which @solarissmoke and @craigloftus already working on.

I totally agree. There are enough indications that a method is async:

1. Return type is Task / Task<>
2. Code completion presents an awaitable hint 
3. the IDE will warn you by underlining in green 
4. Compiler will present a warning when task is not awaited. 

Using the Async suffix only makes sense if your API offers the same functionality in both flavors:

```
GetObject()
GetObjectAsync()
```

For any other use case, using the Async-suffix is as silly as adding a Lazy-suffix to any Lazy<> property:

`public Lazy<Customer> CustomerLazy.`

If you wouldn't do that, then don't use the Async suffix :-)

First off, everything looks correct so you're in good shape :-). Second, I apologize for our versioning and the way it's reported is unclear and confusing. When you do an install there are three components which can version independently: host, runtime/shared framework and the SDK. All of these can live side-by-side. Trying to organize and tell a coherent story with all of these versions floating around is proving very difficult.

Your apt-get install output indicates the packages included in the 1.0.1 SDK are set up. 

```
Setting up dotnet-host (1.1.0-preview1-001100-00-1) ...
Setting up dotnet-hostfxr-1.0.1 (1.0.1-1) ...
Setting up dotnet-sharedframework-microsoft.netcore.app-1.0.4 (1.0.4-1) ...
Setting up dotnet-hostfxr-1.1.0 (1.1.0-1) ...
Setting up dotnet-sharedframework-microsoft.netcore.app-1.1.1 (1.1.1-1) ...
Setting up dotnet-dev-1.0.1 (1.0.1-1) ...
```

`dotnet` will return the Host version (1.1.0) where `dotnet --version` returns the SDK version. If you poke around the directories under /usr/share/dotnet, you'll probably see a number of installed versions. There is policy in the various components to automatically choose the highest patch version within a minor version "family". So if you have 1.0.1, 1.0.3, 1.0.4 under /shared/Microsoft.NETCore.App, 1.0.4 will be used unless you specifically configure a project to use something different. The same rules apply to the other components. 

Hope that brief explanation is helpful.

I've received the same error today, however I haven't changed anything in `/etc/letsencrypt` (the last modification dates are when I last ran `letsencrypt-auto` three days ago).

I think I've found it (very nice piece of software, `ipdb`!):

In [`configurator.py`](https://github.com/letsencrypt/letsencrypt/blob/master/letsencrypt-apache/letsencrypt_apache/configurator.py#L219), `find_dir()` is called as followed:

    self.parser.find_dir("SSLCertificateFile", None, vhost.path)

And its definition is: `def find_dir(self, directive, arg=None, start=None, exclude=True)`

So what I didn't notice with my previous debug attempts was that `exclude` was `true`. For some reason I assumed it was `false` and ignored the `matches = self._exclude_dirs(matches)` part from `find_dir()`.

No with `ipdb` I've learned checks all loaded Apache modules and checks if the directive in question isn't "encased" in an `<IfModule>` which *isn't* loaded..

Now, to the Gentoo-part: as I've made clear many times, Gentoo doesn't have the Debian style `enmod` and `dismod`. It uses `<IfDefine>` sections in the `loadmodule` part of `httpd.conf` and the `-D` switches for those `<IfDefine>`s are added to the command in the configuration file `/etc/conf.d/apache2` of the init script.
The Let's Encrypt client doesn't consider those `-D` switches from the `conf.d` file, so `mod_ssl` is never loaded, as far as Let's Encrypt is concerned. Therefore, `_exclude_dirs()` will return `false` and everything fails..

Design I'm currently working on:

1. letsencrypt-auto bootstraps the OS packages in shell. We inline the scripts in the current `bootstrap` folder, putting each in its own function to avoid a profusion of globals. It'll be no fun to edit. Better ideas welcome.
2. It then transfers control to a heredoc-embedded Python script[1]. The Python script queries GH for the latest LE release version[2], pulls the letsencrypt-auto script of that version, pulls also the signature for that version (a separate file), checks it, and replaces the current local version of letsencrypt-auto if possible (dropping it in a temp file otherwise).
3. The original copy of letsencrypt-auto now runs the new copy, passing it a private flag[3] along with the original args.
4. The new copy of letsencrypt-auto sees the flag and transfers control straight to an embedded Python script (since it knows the OS packages are already there) which embeds peep and a requirements file for the union of LE packages' dependencies. It installs the Python dependencies, checking their hashes.
5. Finally, the new copy of letsencrypt-auto does, probably verbatim, `$SUDO $VENV_BIN/letsencrypt "$@"`, as the current version does.

[1] Advantages: No need to install even more dependencies (curl, zip, tar, etc.), better flow control, and the option of future Windows compatibility. Python makes a good portable "platform", thanks to batteries-included.
[2] Caveat: letsencrypt-auto doesn't work offline. But I don't think it ever did.
[3] Since letsencrypt-auto updates itself before peep is ever run, we solve the problem of peep being potentially incompatible with future pip versions. If we update our embedded version and publish a release, we're good. As a result, we avoid the business of embedding the entirety of pip, a 1.4MB behemoth.

This is probably related to #22, which resolved issue #21. I think that AM/PM is superfluous for any numerical values outside of 0 <= hour <= 12. It's *much* more likely to be an error than a date in that case.

One thing to note is that I have seen in Japan the use of hours >= 24, like a ramen shop might be open 18:00-26:00 (i.e. 2AM). See [this Wikipedia link](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Date_and_time_notation_in_Japan#Time). I think that's *also* more likely than not to be an error, but it seems like another one of those things that should, in the end, be exposed for tinkering in case people need to adapt it to their specific context.

So the current implementation works by overriding the behavior of @get_requirement@, but this only works for leaf nodes and not for overrides applied to workflows or workflow steps.

So either:

1. Apply overrides at load time (this is where the requirements list gets built, so all the changes related to @get_requirement@ can be reverted)
1. Compute requirements at runtime, then apply overrides (requires refactoring)

The benefit of the 2nd approach is that you could, in principal, run multiple instances of the same workflow in the same process, but with different overrides, without loading multiple copies of the workflow/tool files.  But it isn't clear we need that right now, and the refactoring is kind of annoying and will make this feature take longer.

Okay, the problem here is the symbolic initialization. It will initialize the owner as msg.sender, which is an unconstrained symbol. As such its not weird that in the analysis message call, the check "owner == msg.sender" is satisfiable, because owner will also be symbolic.

To solve this we could make the message sender a concrete value. This could roughly look like this:
- create initial "creator" account => creator
- initiate contract creation transaction where origin and sender are creator

```python
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from pvlib.atmosphere import first_solar_spectral_correction, gueymard94_pw

temp_air = np.linspace(-10, 40, 51)
relative_humidity = np.linspace(0, 100, 51)

temps_humids = np.meshgrid(temp_air, relative_humidity)

pws = gueymard94_pw(temps_humids[0], temps_humids[1])

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12,9), subplot_kw=dict(aspect=0.5))
im = ax.contourf(temps_humids[0], temps_humids[1], pws, 50, cmap=plt.get_cmap('viridis'), vmin=0, vmax=5)
ax.set_ylabel('relative humidity (%)')
ax.set_xlabel('temperature (C)')
fig.colorbar(im, label='pw (cm)')

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12,9), subplot_kw=dict(aspect=0.5))
im = ax.contourf(temps_humids[0], temps_humids[1], pws, 50, cmap=plt.get_cmap('viridis'))
ax.set_ylabel('relative humidity (%)')
ax.set_xlabel('temperature (C)')
fig.colorbar(im, label='pw (cm)')

ams = np.linspace(1, 5, 51)
pws = np.linspace(0, 5, 51)
ams, pws = np.meshgrid(ams, pws)

cmap = plt.get_cmap('viridis')
n = 15
vmin = .94
vmax = 1.05

for module_type in ['cdte', 'xsi', 'polysi']:
    mods = first_solar_spectral_correction(pws, ams, module_type=module_type)

    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10,7))
    
    im = ax.contour(ams, pws, mods, n, cmap=cmap, vmin=vmin, vmax=vmax)
    im2 = ax.contourf(ams, pws, mods, n, cmap=cmap, vmin=vmin, vmax=vmax)
    ax.set_ylabel('preciptable water (cm)')
    ax.set_xlabel('airmass')
    ax.clabel(im, colors='k', fmt='%.2f')
    fig.colorbar(im2, label='modifier')
    ax.set_title(module_type)
```

The dropbox SDK (no, I'm absolutely not working on a DropboxFS :smile:) uses the following conventions everytime there's a conflict when trying to either write a file or create a folder (it's not really a Python exceptions scheme because the use Javaesque exceptions):
- `WriteConflict('file')` – There’s a file in the way (that would be `fs.errors.FileExists`)
- `WriteConflict('folder')` – There’s a folder in the way (that would be `fs.errors.DirectoryExists`)
- `WriteConflict('file_ancestor')` – There’s a file at an ancestor path, so we couldn’t create the required (that would be `fs.errors.DirectoryExpected`).

At least that resolves the aforementioned case and makes it clearer, although it's still not perfect since a folder creating function will raise a `FileExists` error.

> Isn't CMP then STRNE is logically similar to cmp and str on separate branch?

You are right, but that is not the only way to implement `CMP + STRNE`. VEX chooses to implement it as a _conditional store_, not a _conditional branch_ where only one branch does the store, as you can see from the lifting result:

```
   13 | ------ IMark(0x400ee8, 4, 0) ------
   14 | t56 = t53
   15 | t31 = Add32(t0,0x00000014)
   16 | if (t56) STle(t31) = t54
```

The (conditional) store will only happen when `t56` is not zero.

> There is no constrain that r0 must not be 0 (as seen on address 0xEE0)

The constraint is the guard of the ITE expression `<BV32 if (reg_r0_3_32 == 0x0) then reg_r3_6_32 else 0x0>` that is stored in memory when executing statement 16 `16 | if (t56) STle(t31) = t54`. Unfortunately you cannot extract that condition from `solver.constraints`, as VEX did not model it as a path predicate that leads to conditional branches.

I guess it's a low level IR? I'm not familiar with the terminology you're using.

that question seems to only makes sense in the context of compiler IRs. VEX is a lifter IR, where the goal is first and foremost to represent the semantics of binary code under a CPU. There is an optimization setting you can specify, but because of the goals of the IR (represent the semantics of a block of code), they are very limited in scope and can't do much besides redundant operation elimination and constant propagation.

From your notes in #1507, the `SELECT COUNT(*)` is able to do a table scan, I guess probably because the vast majority of your DB is buildhub stuff. However, the combined data-and-count is probably doing an index scan so it can output results in the right order, and I bet the index scan adds some overhead. On my machine, the difference is not so drastic -- about a factor of 2x -- but maybe on your machine the table scan is being parallelized in a way that the index scan can't be.

One obvious difference between the queries you're benchmarking and the ones in your PR is that the ones in the PR have filters associated with them. Between this and the fact that your broken-down queries are doing a table scan, it might be good to see what happens if you run the query on a collection that makes up a smaller proportion of the database.

I didn't know about windowing functions. The query I came up with is much closer to the original query:

```
WITH collection_filtered AS (
    SELECT id, last_modified, data, deleted
      FROM records
     WHERE parent_id = '/buckets/a/collections/b'
       AND collection_id = 'record'
  ORDER BY last_modified DESC
),
total_filtered AS (
    SELECT COUNT(*) AS count_total
      FROM collection_filtered
     WHERE NOT deleted
)
 SELECT count_total,
       a.id, as_epoch(a.last_modified) AS last_modified, a.data
  FROM collection_filtered as a,
       total_filtered
 LIMIT 10;
```

- It is true that MP doesn't have a way to pull uniform band structures. I have advocated for this for some time, but it is really something that needs to be coordinated with Materials Project and not matminer. I see you have posted an issue to the MP forum, that is the appropriate venue for the request.
- For BPE with line mode, BPE is intended to take an integral over all of k-space. A line-mode band structure doesn't allow one to do this (this is why one also doesn't compute total energies from line mode calculations). Of course if one wanted very coarse results they could "cheat" and use the line mode, and if you had materials with the same k-path any errors might even be systematic and usable, but I don't think it's worth spending effort coding a method that's the incorrect definition.

I will bring up the uniform band structure issue in my next meeting with MP to see if they can push this forward, that is the proper solution to this rather than redefining the branch point energy to not be a proper integral over k-space. 1. Needs some docs (somewhere)

- Do we really need an option for postprocessing as float vs complex, etc? Can we just postprocess everything as float? Or, if you really want it, put this in the constructor rather than the featurize() method? We are really trying to keep options out of featurize() to the extent possible.

Can we just change this to "don't make me think"? i.e., for formatting, etc., just automatically do what's reasonable. If you run that as a standalone python script (ie, python hoppertest.py) it should close the window when the program exits. It's a limitation of Python that it can't respond to X events while waiting for console input.

BTW, I would like to share my recent experience handling errors with `graphq-java` which I believe is the reference in the JVM world. 

**Error vs Exception**

When using this library, I always try to separate what is an `error` of what is an `exception`. The framework itself sometimes mixes errors and exceptions, but I think there's a simple rule that could be used from the developer point of view that could help when deciding what error policy to apply.

- An `error` is just a data structure carrying data used to rendered as an error as a result of a given business logic. An error it's not returned as result but added to the current execution context errors. That enables all use cases, even the rare use case of having a result with data and errors.

- Whereas an `exception` is a non expected behavior that happens to be rendered as an `error` with the extra information of the exception's stacktrace. When an exception is thrown, the current execution is interrupted and, by default you can see the stacktrace in the logs, because is a non expected behavior. The appealing part of exceptions is that you may think of exceptions as a way of returning an error every time you don't want to return a result. And that's not the best way of building your business logic. At least in the Java world an stacktrace has a given execution cost that a simple data structure doesn't have. That's why `errors` as data are better than **throwing** exceptions. 

If you would like to handle certain situations and send an error back you should be sending an `error` which is an expected result of a certain business logic.

**Error extensions**

An `error` has pretty much the same structure of an exception

- message
- location
- path

But it also has an `extensions` attribute that could be used to add extra information to the current error.

I think the issue with using a summary-time flag is just backwards compatibility.  Users will already have summary data written out that's both supposed to be markdown and supposed to not be markdown.  Right now it all gets interpreted as markdown, annoying the not-supposed-to-be-markdown folks, but if we change the default behavior (in the absence of an explicit summary flag) it would probably annoy the supposed-to-be-markdown folks.

The only way to make TensorBoard work reliably for existing text summary data (or for that matter, for code using existing libraries/helper modules that use text summaries with no flag) would be adding a toggle.

I think a flag at the summary writing time would still make sense, but perhaps more as a default position for the toggle.  FWIW, having a toggle would also be useful to people who are trying to write markdown but where the markdown was misrendered, so that they can see what the actual raw output was.

@jart As discussed in #1202, the issue here is not GraphDef, which is already divided into small chunks prior to sending through gRPC. The issue here is source code and Python tracebacks, which are populated in a single protocol buffer and sent through gRPC. This fails when the size of the protocol buffer is larger than 4 MB, due to, for example, large source files or many of them. The current workaround is using `send_traceback_and_source_code=False` as mentioned above.

Eventually, on Windows, everything external to the core Thread logic, will sit on top of the otApi.dll. This DLL currently exposes nearly every control path API exposed by openthread.h. It purposefully excludes any API that would be socket related, as that is expected and required to use the normal Windows Socket APIs. otApi.dll will implement each control path API and communicate it to the otLwf.sys filter driver via IOCTLs. Depending on the currently connect device, otLwf.sys will then either call OpenThread APIs directly in response to the IOCTL, or encode it with spinel to send directly to the device.

All user experiences and testing go through otApi.dll. The Thread Harness for Windows will have to go through it. Any off-mesh commissioning logic will go through it. On-mesh commissioning logic is considered as part of the core Thread logic, so currently, would be expected to live on device if that is where OpenThread is running.

I never tried to pass `dict`s because I always use schemas. I have no objection to that if it adds flexibility. From a quick glance, I don't see anything blocking this.

Likewise, marshmallow `Schema` instances with parameters should be accepted as well.

In both case, I think it should work already, at least from webargs point of view. However, there may be issues with the documentation. Issues that may lie in apispec rather than flask-rest-api.

If you want to help on this, please be more specific about what you tried, what happened, what you expected,...

One nice feature of flask-rest-api/apispec is the ability to declare schemas (using `definition`) tu re-use them throughout the spec. The matching is done on the schema class, so if you're passing a `Schema` with different parameters to several view functions, and if the schema class is added as a definition, all views will have the schema replaced with a reference to the same class definition, which is wrong. In other words, customizing schemas on-the-fly breaks the definition mechanism. I think this is one of the reason we stayed away from that use case. But if you don't mind losing the definition feature, then I don't see any other blocking issue.

As for recurrent and input terms, I have the plan to implement normalization in _linear. But there is one paper that claims adding BN for input term will hurt generalization performance. 
See http://arxiv.org/abs/1510.01378
It says "We also show that when applied to the input-to-hidden transitions, batch normalization can lead to a faster convergence of the training criterion but doesn't seem to improve the generalization performance on both our language modelling and speech recognition tasks. "

 Adding BN to both will increase computation. Probably we should just try to apply BN to recurrent terms. But I'm not sure about this.
What do you think?

### Benchmark code
I started with [this](http://dask-ml.readthedocs.io/en/latest/examples/hyperparameter-search.html), and made some modifications that I thought would make the benchmarks more comparable:
 - For the grid searches, I ran them 3 times. For the random searches, I upped the count a bit.
 - Actual target of the search was changed from a Regressor to a pipeline containing a StandardScaler so it would have "something" that could theoretically be cached. Not entirely sure my scaling task makes sense.
 - Added fixed values for random_state to the searches and the regressor so that each task was theoretically evaluating the exact same thing.

I didn't put a whole lot more effort into changing the pipeline. I'm not super familiar with classification tasks or this dataset. 

```
if __name__ == '__main__':
    from dask_searchcv import GridSearchCV, RandomizedSearchCV
    from distributed import Client
    from scipy import stats
    from shutil import rmtree
    from sklearn import model_selection as ms
    from sklearn.datasets import load_digits
    from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
    from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline
    from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
    from tempfile import mkdtemp
    from time import time


    def get_clf(random_state, memory_dir=None):
        clf = Pipeline(
                [
                    ('scaler', StandardScaler()),
                    ('clf', LogisticRegression(random_state=random_state)),
                ],
                memory=memory_dir
        )
        return clf


    client = Client()

    # get some data
    digits = load_digits()
    X, y = digits.data, digits.target

    # use a full grid over all parameters
    param_grid = {
        "C": [1e-5, 1e-3, 1e-1, 1],
        "fit_intercept": [True, False],
        "penalty": ["l1", "l2"]
    }
    param_grid = dict([('clf__' + x, y) for x, y in param_grid.items()])

    # scikit-learn
    for i in range(3):
        sk_grid_search = ms.GridSearchCV(get_clf(i), param_grid=param_grid, n_jobs=-1)
        start = time()
        sk_grid_search.fit(X, y)
        print("scikit-learn GridSearchCV took %.2f seconds for %d candidate parameter settings."
              % (time() - start, len(sk_grid_search.cv_results_['params'])))
        del sk_grid_search

    # scikit-learn w/ memory
    for i in range(3):
        tmp_dir = mkdtemp()
        try:
            sk_grid_search_with_memory = ms.GridSearchCV(get_clf(i, tmp_dir), param_grid=param_grid, n_jobs=-1)
            start = time()
            sk_grid_search_with_memory.fit(X, y)

            print("scikit-learn GridSearchCV with memory took %.2f seconds for %d candidate parameter settings."
                  % (time() - start, len(sk_grid_search_with_memory.cv_results_['params'])))
            del sk_grid_search_with_memory
        finally:
            rmtree(tmp_dir)

    # dask
    for i in range(3):
        dk_grid_search = GridSearchCV(get_clf(i), param_grid=param_grid, n_jobs=-1)
        start = time()
        dk_grid_search.fit(X, y)
        print("dashk-searchcv GridSearchCV took %.2f seconds for %d candidate parameter settings."
              % (time() - start, len(dk_grid_search.cv_results_['params'])))
        del dk_grid_search

    param_dist = {
        "C": stats.beta(1, 3),
        "fit_intercept": [True, False],
        "penalty": ["l1", "l2"]
    }
    param_dist = dict([('clf__' + x, y) for x, y in param_dist.items()])

    n_iter_search = 250

    # scikit-learn randomized search
    sk_random_search = ms.RandomizedSearchCV(get_clf(42), param_distributions=param_dist, n_iter=n_iter_search, n_jobs=-1, random_state=42)
    start = time()
    sk_random_search.fit(X, y)
    print("scikit-learn RandomizedSearchCV took %.2f seconds for %d candidates"
          " parameter settings." % ((time() - start), n_iter_search))
    del sk_random_search

    # scikit-learn randomized search with memory
    tmp_dir = mkdtemp()
    try:
        sk_random_search_with_memory = ms.RandomizedSearchCV(
                get_clf(42, tmp_dir), param_distributions=param_dist, n_iter=n_iter_search, n_jobs=-1, random_state=42
        )
        start = time()
        sk_random_search_with_memory.fit(X, y)
        print("scikit-learn RandomizedSearchCV with memory took %.2f seconds for %d candidates"
              " parameter settings." % ((time() - start), n_iter_search))
        del sk_random_search_with_memory
    finally:
        rmtree(tmp_dir)

    # dask randomized search
    dk_random_search = RandomizedSearchCV(get_clf(42), param_distributions=param_dist, n_iter=n_iter_search, n_jobs=-1, random_state=42)
    start = time()
    dk_random_search.fit(X, y)
    print("dashk-searchcv RandomizedSearchCV took %.2f seconds for %d candidates"
          " parameter settings." % ((time() - start), n_iter_search))
    del dk_random_search
```

These are included solely to match the scikit-learn API

copy_x dosen't make sense because dask arrays are immutable. 

n_jobs doesn't really make sense. In scikit-learn, that controls the parallelism of the `n_init` different random initilizations. We only do 1 initialization.

I'm not sure about precompute_distances. I suspect it makes less sense for Dask-ML, since the distances would have to move between workers, but I don't recall the scikit-learn implementation well.

You'll need to provide `__getitem__` and `__setitem__` (Since Python doesn't have references, the case `Foo[x] = y` is special). If you want to wrap an `operator []`, here is an example that might work for `__getitem__`:

```cpp
.def("__getitem__", &Foo::operator []);
```

And, in case it doesn't, this lambda function will work:

```cpp
.def("__getitem__",[](Foo& self, long i) {return self[i];});
```

You'll have to use the lambda function approach for `__setitem__`:

```cpp
.def("__setitem__",[](Foo& self, long i, T value) {self[i] = value;});
```

Where T is the type for your value in the vector.

Function arguments (in contrast to return values) will never be moved by pybind11. You'll have to write your own wrappers as you did above to accomplish this.

This *will* create issues with in combination with default arguments -- as in Python (and unlike C++), they are constructed *once* at declaration time. If the function subsequently moves the argument, this will lead to changes that are visible in later function calls.

For a concrete fix, I propose adding the following to the top of `FindPythonLibsNew.cmake`:
```cmake
if(PYTHONLIBS_FOUND)
    return()
endif()
```

So if the user supplies `PYTHONLIBS_FOUND`, the search is skipped and it's the user's responsibility to supply the required variables.

Example, if you start slapd all the entries in ou=people doesn't have the memberOf attribute.
If you change/create a entry in ou=groups (memberOf) the previous attribute appears in all the users. If you remove/change the memberOf attribute in a ou=people entry this doesn't affect ou=groups entry.

This mean that DIT is hierarchical, changes only applies from top to bottom.

So I never wrtie/change/create memberOf attribute directly in ou=people entries and also never trust the dynamically loaded/updates related variables in ou=people entries regarding ou=group memberships.

I use this method, in LdapUser model (ou=people) to query ou=group entries ad then get the membership of the LdapUser. I use MultiValueField proposed in PR 152, it works like ListValueField but different in representation.

````
    def membership(self):
        if self.member_of: return self.member_of
        # memberOf fill fields in people entries only if a change/write happens in its definitions
        membership = LdapGroup.objects.filter(member__contains=self.dn)
        if membership: return os.linesep.join([i.member for i in membership])
        else: return ''
````

It looks like Certbot is unable to detect ipv6only directive completely for some reason. Removing that would fix the issue for *most* of the users. This can cause some issues with really old Nginx versions, as the behavior of ipv6only and the defaults have changed over the time.

This problem is live in `certbot-0.25.1-3.fc28.noarch`

In `httpd.conf` (which on my system is very nonstandard)

```
<IfModule mod_ssl.c>
   Include "conf/vhosts.ssl.conf"
</IfModule>
```

is morphed by `certbot renew --apache` into

```
<IfModule mod_ssl.c>
   Listen 443
Include "conf/vhosts.ssl.conf"
</IfModule>
```

upon which restart of httpd breaks.

Hi Scott, my apologies for not getting earlier to the subject for I was vacationing. Your reasoning makes perfectly sense but I'd like to hear your opinion on a further refinement.

The "normal" application flow usin on-connect is, as in your case, to do business with the tag within the callback function and return a true value for the presence check to run until the the user removes the tag. The "alternative" flow is to return a false value and have a presence check run as application logic or just quit for other business. I would think of the automatic beep to be needed only in the first case and, for the alternative case, let the application logic decide on whether to use or not use the beep function explicitly. This would mean that the buzzer and led are only to be used if on-connect returns True, i.e right before and after the presence loop.
I would say that it would help future maintainers to add a comment explaining why this is important.

Just to recap, because I keep on misunderstanding this issue every time I try to reread it...

I incorrectly thought the proposed strict mode would require the factors to match *only* a single env. eg, you couldn't just provide `linux`, but instead would have to fully specify `linux-spam-py36`. This "fixes" the issue, because `linus` isn't a valid factor, and no single env would have been matched.

What is actually proposed is that the parsed CI environment factors *must* match variants defined in the tox-travis config. In your example, the tox.ini is misconfigured in that `linus` is not a valid os. The `linux` os factor will be parsed on Travis, but will not match `linus`, and an exception will be raised by tox-travis.

Hopefully I understand it this time 😄.

```yml

to explain for future reference what i've done with the allowed failures in the travis config:

Testing glove + charngram is pretty slow, since it takes awhile to download and unzip the vectors. As a result, I set them as "allowed failures" --- if all the other tests pass, CI will go green. However, these allowed failures will still run, and coverage for the commit will be updated when they finish. This lets us have test w2v / charngram vectors + accurate coverage reporting + still maintain speedy build times for PRs where we don't even touch vector behavior.

I think using pssh is the way to go. Assuming you have a `workers.txt` with all of the node IP addresses (other than the head node), you can do the following. The instructions are similar to the ones in this file https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/master/doc/using-ray-on-a-large-cluster.md and maybe should be added to that file.

1. Stop, update, and start Ray on the head node.

```
# Stop Ray
ray/scripts/stop_ray.sh

# Update Ray
cd ~/ray/python
git pull
python setup.py install

# Start Ray
cd ~
ray/scripts/start_ray.sh --head --num-workers=10 --redis-port=6379
```

Then make a script to run via parallel ssh on all the other nodes. E.g., `script.sh`

```
export PATH=/home/ubuntu/anaconda2/bin/:$PATH
ray/scripts/stop_ray.sh
cd ~/ray/python
git pull
python setup.py install
cd ~
ray/scripts/start_ray.sh --head --num-workers=10 --redis-port=6379
```

Then run it via parallel-ssh

```
parallel-ssh -h workers.txt -P -I < script.sh
```

This assumes you have a `workers.txt` file containing the (private) IP addresses of all the nodes other than the head node and can ssh to the other nodes from the head node.

This is because the backend assumes that every handle is assigned a unique ID, but `new_handle1` and `new_handle2` share the same handle ID, the one stored in the serialized `handle_id`.

A temporary workaround is to assign a random handle ID every time we deserialize from `handle_id`. However, this will become an issue for actor reconstruction. For example, a task that submits methods to an actor might get reconstructed, and we want to make sure that the submitted methods don't execute twice.

Eventually, it seems like we need to store some mutable information about how many times the handle has been deserialized. That information will probably have to go in the GCS since we can't rely on plasma for object consistency. We should be careful about how we expose this API since the `ray.put` and/or `ray.get` operations may not be as cheap as they seem.

1. I don't recommend using the `simulate_boundary_layer` option. Then only reason it is present is to expose all functionality available in the emanuel fortran code. It modifies arrays in-place, and this generally makes it hard to keep track of what is happening. The simple physics package boundary layer code is a simple diffusive boundary layer scheme, and using it keeps the boundary layer and convection components separate.

2. I'm not sure what you expect from the humidity profiles. If you want to specify a humidity profile that triggers convection, you have to calculate the convective available potential energy (CAPE) for the given profiles of temperature and humidity. If this is positive, the emanuel scheme should kick in to remove the CAPE and stabilise the column. IMO, it is simply better to let the boundary layer scheme take care of this, unless you have a very specific profile (like something from an observational campaign) that you want to
use.

I'm still a little hazy on how grids are going to be handled in sympl 0.4, so I'm not sure what you mean that the user specifies the grid manually. My understanding was that there was some standard way to allowing the user to add grids and additional information (like area weights) to sympl, and this would get propagated to the model code that follows.

If that is not the case, then for the moment, I would suggest leaving the area_weights attribute (maybe use underscores to warn the user not to use it) and add a helper function that returns the area weights given a dynamical core. We could worry about this at a later time.

An additional consideration, however: If we switch the array ordering, we can no longer use prange to iterate over columns, because columns will no longer be contiguous in memory. At its core, this is because the dynamical core does not want columns to be contiguous in memory. I think the real solution to this is to look for a way to do parallelism other than prange (for example, by breaking the model into subdomains that are each running an MPI process that corresponds to the run script).

> I am thinking along the lines of long running WebSockets where I am not too worried about losing a few messages but rather several hours of accumulated state. A largely "in memory" scenario with lazy persistence for performance reasons. Being able to save a snapshot on disconnect is definitely worth some complexity vs potentially replaying hours of logs/journals.

I'm dubious about the wisdom of getting into the state where you have hours of valuable data accumulated in memory in the first place – maybe you should flush snapshots regularly on a timer? But that said, assuming there's a good reason to do things this way, I think you'd really want to implement this as a task that listens for control-C and then flushes a snapshot. There's nothing wrong with doing that if you have needs that are a little more unusual/extreme, and I think "I have hours of valuable data that I need to make sure get flushed to a database over an async connection while shutting down" counts.

> Do we also need to worry about partial receives when canceling? I could see potentially getting out of sync if we are expecting an ack as part of our shutdown. Is there some way to cancel only if the receive is idle?

Yeah, you'd have to be careful that the code you put inside the `soft_shield_disabled` block (like `ws_conn.next_message` in the example above) is written in a cancellation-safe manner. So for example if it looks like:
```python
    async def next_message(self):
        while True:
            message = self._proto_state.get_next_message()
            if message is not None:
                return message
            else:
                # Receive some data and then try again
                data = await self._transport.recv(BUFSIZE)
                self._proto_state.receive_bytes(data)
```
then we're pretty much OK – the only `await` is in the call to `self._transport.recv`, and if it raises `Cancelled` then we'll exit early and leave behind any data that we already received in `_proto_state`'s internal buffer to finish reading next time. So as long `recv` itself is [cancellation safe](https://trio.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference-core.html#cancellation-and-primitive-operations) then `next_message` will be too. And `SocketType.recv` is cancellation-safe.

However... this gets tricky for some protocols.

If `self._transport` is an SSL-wrapped socket instead of a regular socket, then it's possible that calling `SSLStream.recv` will need to send data on the underlying socket (because of a horrible thing called renegotiation). The version of `SSLStream` in my branch (#107) definitely does not guarantee that the connection is still usable after `recv` is cancelled.

A similar issue arises at the protocol level. For websockets, the `next_message` method looks to the user like it's just reading, but if it ends up reading a ping frame then it might want to send back a pong. And then if this gets cancelled half-way through then again we've lost sync.

Solving these problems seems really hard :-(. The way these protocols work, once you start trying to send something then you're really committed to doing so – like you just can't stop in the middle of a websocket Pong frame and switch to a Close frame. (For OpenSSL you're even committing to retrying the same OpenSSL API call repeatedly; it's not enough to make sure that the bytes it gave you eventually get sent. But let's ignore that for right now.)

So what would be annoying but possible is to switch the sending to always use a `send`-style API call (which can do partial sends and reports how much data was sent) instead of a `sendall`-call, and only remove bytes from the internal protocol object's outgoing buffer when `send` accepts them. (I wasn't planning to provide `send` in the high-level stream interface at all but let's assume we have it.) This would have the effect that if one operation gets interrupted during a send, then the next operation on the same object would pick it up and finish it before doing whatever else it needs to do. So e.g. if we get cancelled in the middle of sending a Ping, then when we go to send the Close it'll send the second half of the Ping + the Close.

Possibly this would become simpler if we change the semantics of `sendall` (in the high level stream interface, not for the raw socket interface) so that on cancellation it holds onto the data and automatically prepends it to the next call to `sendall`? I'm wary because this moves us back towards the infinite buffers of twisted-like systems and somewhat violates the no-implicit-concurrency rule, but if it's restricted to cancellations, where currently the only thing you can do is just close the connection, then maybe it's good. Basically this would be implementing the buffering logic inside the stream object instead of the protocol object. ...though, maybe it wouldn't work with SSL, because we could get in a state where we need to call `sendall(b"")` to flush out the internal send buffer before anything will work, but we don't know it and block waiting to receive instead.

------

Another possibility that we add more shielding states. Specifically, a task could be in the four states listed down the left column here, and the table shows what checkpoints do in each case:

|                                | Soft-cancelled            | Hard-cancelled    |
|--------------------------------|---------------------------|-------------------|
| Default                        | raise `Cancelled`         | raise `Cancelled` |
| Soft-shield, exceptions OK     | raise `CancelledIsComing` | raise `Cancelled` |
| Soft-shield, exceptions not OK | nothing                   | raise `Cancelled` |
| Hard shield                    | nothing                   | nothing           |

But we don't set these states directly; instead, there's are two context managers: one that marks its contents as "soft-cancellation enabled", and one that marks its contents as "state may become corrupted if a cancellation happens". Then the states are:

* if there are any `shield=True` scopes on the stack below the cancelled scope, then we're in state "Hard shield"

* else, if there is at least one "soft-cancellation enabled" manager in effect, and no "state may become corrupted if a cancellation happens" managers in effect, then we're in state "Soft-shield, exceptions OK"

* else, if there is at least one "soft-cancellation enabled" manager in effect, and at least one "state may become corrupted if a cancellation happens" managers in effect, then we're in state "Soft-shield, exceptions not OK"

* else, we're in state "Default"

So... this is really complicated. But the idea is that in the end, a method like `SSLStream.recv` puts the "state may become corrupted" manager around its calls to `sendall`, and by default this doesn't do anything, but now it can guarantee that if some code that supports soft-cancellation calls it, and a soft-cancellation happens, then `SSLStream.recv` will leave the stream in a consistent state.

@oremanj Sorry for being so slow to review this! This is going to be kind of a combined review of this (#16) and #17...

So first of all, I want to say again how incredibly impressive this work is on the technical level! I am in awe.

If I understand correctly, the pitch for this PR is "you can use `yield_from_` without having to type `@async_generator`", and the pitch for #17 is "async generators get several times faster and their tracebacks stop being so incredibly terrible".

If I have the right, then I don't this PR is worth it. It's not that hard to write `@async_generator`, right? And whenever people skip writing it, it makes their code incompatible with PyPy, and also dependent on CPython implementation details in a way that could cause it to break in the future. That seems like a bad trade-off.

OTOH, #17 is somewhat attractive, if we can make it essentially transparent, so people don't write their code any differently but just magically get better runtime behavior. The tracebacks are *really* bad. I guess I am particularly annoyed at them because of how trio's nursery implementation uses `@async_generator` + `@asynccontextmanager` and that makes like, every trio traceback terrible. We shouldn't weight that *too* highly, since we need to rewrite the nursery to stop using `@asynccontextmanager` anyway (https://github.com/python-trio/trio/issues/393), but still, the tracebacks are bad.

It's such an intrusive change that it does make me nervous – `async_generator` gets [several thousand downloads a day now](https://pypistats.org/packages/async-generator) – but if it can be done in a seamless kind of way where it "just works" and no-one notices, then that'd be pretty cool actually. As written #17 isn't like that yet, with the deprecations and everything, and I'm not sure whether it's actually possible or not (combining bytecode introspection *and* rewriting code objects *and* ctypes leaves a lot of room for things to go subtly wrong!), but if you want to convince me I'm open to being convinced?

That's really odd. The bug seems to stem from `chardet` when `requests` tries to automagically determine the character encoding of the response. This is actually absolutely unnecessary since the response can be quite safely assumed to be in UTF-8 (if not straight ASCII). At least the XML-encoded responses include `<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>` and this most probably applies for JSON responses as well.

So a simple fix (and a minor performance improvement) would be to replace every `response.text` with `response.content.decode("utf8")`. Making sure it works correctly on Python 2 as well will be a minor source of pain here, though.

@jorgemf The main purpose of this PR is to help users to automatically setup the TF_CONFIG environment for parallel TF programs locally.  We have run the inception v3 example on a Azure GPU 4 card VM using asynchronous synchronization. We observe that the GPUs utilization is around 90%. Each worker is processing their data shard at almost equivalent rate. This means that for GPU-intensive model, we could expect performance speed up more or less, though we do admit that the fact that gRPC is not very efficient for big models. As for the model convergence time, we believe this would depends on the model, data shading, as well as the synchronization approach. We regards this as a separated issue which shall be explored by the users themselves.
